dimensionSummaries.title: Authentication & Session Security
dimensionSummaries.summary: The core JWT mechanics are sound for a 30-person hobby app: signing/verification is pinned to HS256 with an explicit algorithms allowlist (no "none"-alg risk), expiry is enforced, passwords use bcrypt, and the magic-link flow is genuinely well-built (crypto-random token, sha256-at-rest, single-use, 15-min TTL, prior-token revocation, rate limiting, no raw-token logging). Admin endpoints uniformly require AdminUser. The real exposures are concentrated in three areas. (1) Registration is fully open self-signup with no invite/approval gate and the active competition is resolved globally — so anyone who can reach /api/auth/register joins the live prediction pool; combined with there being no admin-bootstrap path in the repo, the launch posture and first-admin story are undefined. (2) The JWT secret has a weak, well-known committed default in both docker-compose and .env.example with zero startup validation — if it ships unset, every token (including is_admin) is forgeable, which is a direct cheat/takeover vector. (3) Google OAuth runs with no CSRF state parameter (fastapi-sso defaults to requires_state=False and the code never opts in), and the callback returns the JWT in a URL query string. Token storage in localStorage is the standard SvelteKit tradeoff and acceptable here, but worth noting. Severity is calibrated to the friend-group threat model: the open-registration + weak-secret combination is what could actually let a curious friend cheat or impersonate, so those lead.
strengths: Magic-link service is textbook: secrets.token_urlsafe(32) entropy, sha256 hash at rest (raw token never stored or logged), single-use via used_at set in the same transaction as lookup (concurrent-verify safe), 15-min TTL, prior-unused-token revocation, and a 3-per-15-min rate limit (magic_link.py:88-329).
JWT verification pins the algorithm allowlist to [settings.jwt_algorithm] (HS256) in jwt.decode (dependencies.py:69), closing the classic alg=none / RS256→HS256 confusion attacks.

JWT expiry is set on every token (create_access_token, dependencies.py:49) and validated by python-jose's default exp check; get_current_user also re-checks is_active on every request (dependencies.py:82-83), so deactivated users are locked out immediately despite stateless tokens.
Passwords are hashed with bcrypt + per-hash gensalt() (dependencies.py:33-38); login and password-change use constant-comparison checkpw and never echo password state.
Login returns an identical generic 'Invalid email or password' for both unknown-user and wrong-password (auth.py:86-96), avoiding credential-stuffing enumeration on the password path.
All /api/admin/* routes consistently depend on AdminUser (admin.py), and the UserCreate schema exposes only email/name/password — no mass-assignment of is_admin/is_active/paid on registration.
Password change correctly returns 403 for non-EMAIL (Google) accounts and requires the current password (auth.py:206-218).

dimensionSummaries.title: Authorization, IDOR & Blind-Pool Enforcement
dimensionSummaries.summary: Authorization is, with two notable exceptions, in good shape for a 30-person private app. Every prediction write path (match single/batch, bracket rewrite, bonus) is scoped server-side to current_user.id and never trusts a user_id from the body or path — there is no IDOR on mutations, and no mass-assignment surface (paid/is_admin/points/user_id are absent from every request schema). All admin operations in admin.py, plus admin mutations in fixtures.py and scores.py, are gated by a real AdminUser dependency that checks current_user.is_admin server-side, not just hidden from the nav. The match-score blind pool is enforced correctly on the server in two places (predictions /matches/{id}/community and users /{id}/predictions for match rows). HOWEVER, there is one genuine server-side blind-pool hole that lets a curious friend read every opponent's full bracket (group winners, knockout advancement, predicted champion) before Phase 1 locks: the bracket_summary half of GET /users/{user_id}/predictions returns ALL of the target user's TeamPredictions with no lock/visibility check, and the profile page renders them under a "Locked predictions" label. Secondary issues: an unauthenticated POST /leaderboard/invalidate, and the absence of any DB-level uniqueness on prediction rows (the model's Config.unique_together is a SQLModel no-op), which threatens the 100%-integrity invariant under concurrent batch writes.
strengths: All prediction mutations (predictions.py: PUT /matches/{id}, POST /matches/batch, PUT /bracket, POST /bonus) derive ownership from current_user.id and filter both reads and writes by it — a client cannot target another user's row by passing a foreign user_id; there is no IDOR on any write path.

Request schemas are tight against mass assignment: MatchPredictionCreate/Update, BracketPredictionUpdate, BonusPredictionUpdate, ScoreUpdate and UserCreate contain only the safe fields — none accept paid, is_admin, is_active, points, score, or user_id, so a client cannot escalate privilege or set their own points via a request body (schemas/prediction.py, schemas/auth.py, schemas/score.py).

Every admin endpoint (admin.py users list, toggle admin/active/paid, phase ops, scores/sync, bonus answers, audit history) and every admin mutation in fixtures.py and scores.py uses the AdminUser dependency, which enforces current_user.is_admin server-side (dependencies.py:102-111) — authorization is not merely hidden in the frontend nav.
The audit-log endpoints (admin/users/{user_id}/history) are correctly behind AdminUser, so a non-admin friend cannot pull another player's prediction-change history for dispute snooping.

The match-score blind pool is enforced on the server with the correct lock semantics: get_community_predictions (predictions.py:585) and the match-prediction loop in get_user_predictions (users.py:213-217) both 403/skip until get_fixture_lock_view reports locked or the fixture is finished, including the Phase-1-deadline rule for group fixtures.

The /predictions/agreements endpoint is carefully designed to leak nothing pre-lock: it returns only aggregate counts computed relative to the caller's own pick, never another user's individual prediction (predictions.py:826-881).

Phase separation is respected on the bracket write path: the destructive delete-then-insert in PUT /bracket is scoped to (user_id, current_phase), so a Phase 2 rewrite cannot wipe Phase 1 picks (predictions.py:527-531).

dimensionSummaries.title: Input Validation, Injection & XSS
dimensionSummaries.summary: The backend is in good shape against injection and XSS for a 30-person hobby app. Every DB query uses SQLModel/SQLAlchemy expression builders with bound parameters — there is no raw SQL, no text(), no f-string/format-built queries anywhere, so SQL injection is not reachable. The frontend uses Svelte, which auto-escapes all {expression} interpolation; the dozen {@html} sinks I traced are all fed either static template strings, server-derived integers (the caller's own data), or a bundled static SVG map — none render another user's free-text (name, bonus answer). User-supplied strings that DO reach HTML email bodies are escaped via _esc. OAuth/magic-link redirect targets come from server config, not user-supplied next/returnTo params, so there's no open redirect. The real gaps are write-path validation completeness on the two free-text prediction surfaces: the bracket endpoint stores arbitrary unbounded team/stage strings with no allowlist and no list-size cap, and the bonus endpoint stores unbounded-length answers with no batch-size cap. These are data-integrity / storage-abuse issues, not cheats — advancement scoring gates on teams that actually progressed, so forged team/stage values earn zero points. Score inputs ARE bounded server-side (0-20), so negative/huge goal values are rejected; the only nit is the cap is 20 while the UI says 15.
strengths: No raw SQL anywhere: every query in services and api uses SQLModel select()/delete()/update() with bound parameters; grep for text(), .execute("..."), f"SELECT", .format(), and exec_driver_sql found zero query-building occurrences. SQL injection is not reachable.

Blind pool is enforced server-side, not just in the UI: get_community_predictions (api/predictions.py:585) and get_user_predictions (api/users.py:213-217) both check get_fixture_lock_view / MatchStatus.FINISHED before returning any other user's picks.

All {@html} Svelte sinks were traced to safe sources: DwHighlights/DwFunnelHero/DwMemorialStrip receive server-derived integers from the caller's own /me/highlights data (e.g. user_pick = f"{home}-{away}"), PnFlag renders a bundled static SVG keyed by team code, and DwChampionPodium renders user names via plain {name} (auto-escaped), not @html.

Magic-link HTML email (services/magic_link.py:_esc) and the Phase 1 receipt email (services/receipts.py:_esc) HTML-escape every user-supplied field (user.name, team names, bonus answers) before interpolating into the HTML body.

No open redirect: the Google OAuth callback redirects to settings.cors_origins[0] (server config) and the magic-link URL is built from settings.public_base_url — neither uses a user-supplied next/returnTo/redirect parameter.
Email header injection is not possible: subjects are static server strings, recipients are validated EmailStr, and Resend is called with a JSON body (no SMTP header concatenation).

Score write path is bounded server-side: MatchPredictionCreate/Update enforce Field(ge=0, le=20) and ScoreUpdate enforces ge=0, so negative and absurdly large goal counts are rejected at the schema layer regardless of the UI cap.

JWT uses a fixed single-element algorithm list (algorithms=[settings.jwt_algorithm], HS256) so alg=none confusion is not possible, and jwt_secret_key is a required setting with no insecure default. yaml.safe_load is used for config; no eval/exec/pickle/template-injection patterns exist in the backend.

dimensionSummaries.title: Infrastructure, Config, Headers & Secrets
dimensionSummaries.summary: For a 30-friend hobby app behind Cloudflare Tunnel, the infra/config posture is mostly reasonable: secrets come from env (no committed .env, sane .gitignore), DB and backend ports bind to 127.0.0.1 only, /docs is gated behind DEBUG, admin endpoints are uniformly AdminUser-gated, magic-link tokens are crypto-random/hashed/single-use/rate-limited, and nginx sets nosniff + X-Frame-Options + per-IP rate limiting with correct real-IP recovery. The most serious issues are operational/launch-time, not deep design flaws: the single docker-compose.yml runs the backend with `--reload` + source bind-mounts even under the `prod` profile (dev server in prod), and JWT_SECRET_KEY / DATABASE_PASSWORD silently fall back to well-known hardcoded defaults if the prod .env omits them — a JWT secret of "super-secret-dev-key-change-in-prod" lets any friend forge an admin token, which is the single highest-impact finding for this threat model. Secondary: the email/password /login and /magic-link/verify paths have no per-account brute-force throttle (only a coarse 10 r/s per-IP nginx limit and a 3-per-15min email-send cap), Google-OAuth exception text and magic-link error detail are reflected to the client, and there's no HSTS/CSP/Referrer-Policy. None of the gaps are enterprise-overkill flags; they map directly to cheat/takeover/snoop risks or launch reliability.
strengths: No .env committed; only backend/.env.example is tracked. .gitignore correctly ignores .env, *.env, *.pem, *.key, credentials.json, *.db.

DB and backend host ports bind to loopback only ("127.0.0.1:5432:5432", "127.0.0.1:8000:8000") in docker-compose.yml, so Postgres/backend aren't directly reachable from the network even before the Cloudflare Tunnel.
FastAPI /api/docs and /api/redoc are gated behind settings.debug (main.py:36-37), so OpenAPI is not exposed when DEBUG is false.
Every admin endpoint is uniformly protected with the AdminUser dependency (admin.py — _admin: AdminUser on all 15 routes); no admin operation is reachable without is_admin.

Magic-link auth is well designed: secrets.token_urlsafe(32), sha256-hashed at rest, 15-min TTL, single-use with atomic used_at, prior-token revocation, and a 3-per-15min per-account send cap (magic_link.py).
Passwords are bcrypt-hashed with per-hash salt (dependencies.py:33-38); registration enforces min_length=8 (schemas/auth.py).

nginx sets X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN, has per-real-IP rate limiting (limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr 10r/s burst=20) and correctly recovers the real client IP from CF-Connecting-IP only from private ranges (nginx.conf:31-40).
CORS allow_origins is an explicit env-driven allowlist (no "*"), which is the correct pairing with allow_credentials=True.
Daily encrypted offsite DB backups to R2 with 14-day retention and fail-loud cron semantics (ops/backup-db.sh).

dimensionSummaries.title: Business-Logic Integrity (locking, phases, scoring, time)
dimensionSummaries.summary: The core write-path locking model is genuinely well built: every prediction-mutation endpoint (single match, batch, bracket rewrite, bonus) re-checks the lock server-side using utc_now() before writing, derives prediction phase from the immutable fixture.stage rather than mutable global state, and the phase1-deadline-vs-per-match-T-lock gap is explicitly closed and regression-tested (test_predictions_lock_guards.py). Phase 1/2 storage and queries are cleanly separated, Phase 2 activation and all score mutation are admin-gated, scoring is computed 100% server-side from DB rows with no user-influenceable input, and the rarity bonus uses per-fixture predictor counts derived from stored predictions. The UTC-datetime invariant holds across the hot paths. The serious gap is on the READ side of the blind pool: while the dedicated community/profile endpoints correctly gate on lock state, the /predictions/agreements endpoint exposes per-fixture agreement counts for ANY fixture with no lock check, which a technical friend can use to reconstruct other players' un-locked predictions; and the per-user predictions endpoint leaks bracket picks unconditionally even though it gates match predictions. These are the launch-blocking items.
strengths: Write-path lock is enforced server-side with utc_now() on every mutation endpoint (update_match_prediction, batch_update_predictions, update_bracket_predictions, save_bonus_predictions) — never trusts client time, and there is no backdating/replay vector because lock state is recomputed from fixture.kickoff each request (predictions.py:212-223, 311-327, 490-499, 694-698).

The phase1_deadline-vs-per-match-T-lock gap is explicitly understood and closed: group fixtures lock en-masse at phase1_deadline so a user cannot edit a group score in the window between the Phase 1 deadline and that match's own kickoff (locking.py:86-120, predictions.py:208-216), with dedicated regression tests in test_predictions_lock_guards.py.

Prediction phase is derived from the immutable fixture.stage, not the mutable global get_current_phase, preventing a Phase-1 group prediction from being mis-tagged Phase 2 (predictions.py:234-242) — and this is regression-tested (TestPredictionPhaseDerivation).

Phase 1 and Phase 2 predictions are stored and queried separately throughout (bracket rewrite deletes/inserts scoped to one phase only — predictions.py:525-531; scoring sums into independent PhaseBreakdown buckets — scoring.py:636-666).

Scoring is entirely server-side and deterministic: points come only from stored MatchPrediction/TeamPrediction/Score/BonusAnswer rows; the rarity/hybrid bonus is computed from per-fixture predictor counts the server tallies itself (scoring.py:618-622, 692-710); there is no endpoint that lets a user submit or influence their own point total.

External score ingestion and all score/phase mutations are admin- or scheduler-only (AdminUser dependency on /scores/sync, /scores PUT, phase2 activate/deactivate, phase1 deadline; dependencies.py:102-111). A normal user cannot trigger sync or spoof a result.

Score sync and snapshot writes are idempotent (sync_scores_once re-run yields zero new rows; take_daily_snapshots uses ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING on a per-user-per-day unique constraint — snapshots.py:64-72), so repeated scheduler ticks cannot corrupt or double-count.

The UTC-aware-datetime invariant is respected on every business-logic path: utc_now() is used for all lock comparisons, token expiry, and timestamps; the only datetime.now()/date.today() occurrences are either equivalent (tz-aware UTC) or cosmetic.
Score.outcome correctly resolves knockout results via penalties then extra-time then regular time (score.py:49-71), so KO advancement and outcome scoring honour the real winner.

dimensionSummaries.title: Backend Performance & Caching (10-20 concurrent users)
dimensionSummaries.summary: The leaderboard is the dominant performance risk. Its cold-path recomputation is a deeply nested N+1 explosion: for each active user it re-scans all match predictions, runs one `get_outcome_counts` query per finished fixture, recomputes `get_actual_advancement` and the entire group-standings + third-place + FIFA-rankings chain (the latter computed two-to-three times per user), and recomputes bonus points. With the full 104-match group stage scored and 30 users, a single cold build is on the order of several thousand SQL round-trips. A 30s in-memory TTL masks this for steady-state reads, but it is unprotected against cache stampede (the public, unauthenticated `/leaderboard/` endpoint with `refresh=true` bypasses the cache entirely and forces a full recompute on demand), and the same recompute is reused by snapshots, trajectory, profile, highlights, and (per-user, in a loop) the deadline receipt batch. None of these are blocking-driver or sync-IO issues — the async DB driver (asyncpg) and httpx are used correctly, email is HTTP-based, and config is LRU-cached — so the problem is purely query volume and recompute amplification, which is tolerable at 30 users only because of the cache and because the heaviest paths fire mostly post-group-stage. There are also a handful of true per-row N+1 loops in prediction/profile list endpoints and a couple of missing composite indexes on hot filter columns. Severity is calibrated down for a 30-person hobby app behind Cloudflare, but the leaderboard recompute cost and stampede exposure are real and worth addressing before the group stage finishes.
strengths: Leaderboard results are cached per-phase with a 30s TTL and explicitly invalidated on score sync and bonus-answer changes (services/score_sync.py:121, api/admin.py:622), so steady-state reads are cheap.
Single uvicorn worker in both dev compose and the Dockerfile (no --workers flag), so the per-process in-memory leaderboard cache is consistent — no cross-worker staleness bug at current scale.

Async DB driver (asyncpg) and async httpx are used throughout the request path; email is a non-blocking HTTP call to Resend with its own retry/backoff, and the heavy email batch runs in the background scheduler tick, never on a user request.
Settings and tournament YAML config are @lru_cache'd (config.py:82,100), so scoring config is not re-parsed from disk on every scoring call.

Several aggregation endpoints were deliberately written to avoid N+1: the roster (api/users.py:125-141) and receipt idempotency pre-load (services/receipts.py:500) use single GROUP BY / IN queries with explanatory comments.

FK and hot single-column indexes exist where expected: match_predictions.user_id/fixture_id, team_predictions.user_id, scores.fixture_id (unique), fixtures.kickoff, plus the snapshot (user_id, captured_date) unique constraint backs idempotent daily writes.
Daily snapshot write uses INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (services/snapshots.py:65-69), so the per-minute scheduler tick is a cheap idempotent no-op after the first call of the day.
The score scheduler gates external API polling behind a cheap has_active_or_imminent_match() check (services/score_sync.py:42), avoiding wasted work and API quota outside match windows.

dimensionSummaries.title: Frontend Performance & Responsiveness
dimensionSummaries.summary: For a 30-person hobby app behind Cloudflare, the frontend is generally well-built and proportionate: stores are module-level singletons that cache across navigation, the results page memoizes per-fixture breakdowns, the localStorage draft mirror is debounced and deduped, fonts use display=swap with preconnect (minimal layout shift), and code-splitting keeps the auth pages free of heavy chunks. Lists (leaderboard ~30 rows, roster ~30, bracket 63 matches) are small enough that NOT virtualizing them is the correct call. The real defects are concentrated in two areas: (1) bundle weight — the PnFlag component eagerly inlines all ~260 flag-icons SVGs as raw strings into the JS bundle on any flag-using route, far more than the ~50 ever shown at once; and (2) the leaderboard live-poll, which every 60s silently resets the user's Phase I/II tab back to "Overall" and flashes a loading state. Several routes also refetch all data on every navigation with no staleness guard, which is wasteful but cheap at this scale. None of this touches the security/cheating/snooping threat model — these are purely sluggishness/UX issues. No data-integrity or blind-pool problems were found on the frontend performance surface.
strengths: Stores are module-level singletons (frontend/src/lib/stores/*.ts), so fetched data (fixtures, predictions, leaderboard) persists across route navigation rather than being lost on unmount — the building block for caching is in place.

Results page precomputes a per-fixture MatchBreakdown Map ONCE per data change (results/+page.svelte:76-90) instead of recomputing inline per render when sort/filter flips — an explicit, well-documented memoization.

The localStorage unsaved-draft mirror (unsavedPersistence.ts) is debounced at 300ms and per-key deduped with a stringify cache, avoiding write storms on every keystroke and breaking the cross-tab echo loop.
Fonts load with display=swap plus preconnect to fonts.gstatic.com (app.html:10-12), so there's no FOIT and minimal layout shift.
Code-splitting works in the app's favor: login/register/auth pages don't import PnFlag or any heavy chrome, so the flag bundle only loads on routes that actually show flags.

Polling and the 1s countdown ticker are both correctly torn down: leaderboard onDestroy calls stopPolling() (leaderboard/+page.svelte:33-35) and the currentTime readable returns a clearInterval cleanup (phase.ts:25) — no obvious interval leaks.
Lists are intentionally NOT virtualized, which is the right proportionate choice for ~30 rows / 63 fixed bracket matches; virtualization would be needless complexity here.

The dashboard dispatcher uses <svelte:component> rather than an if-chain to avoid re-evaluating every branch on uxPhase change (+page.svelte:14-20), and uxPhase is deliberately decoupled from the heavy fixtures writable to avoid a reactive cascade (phase.ts:79-140).

dimensionSummaries.title: Dead Code, Unused Assets & Dev→Prod Cleanliness
dimensionSummaries.summary: The codebase is, on the whole, unusually clean for a hobby project: the backend app/ has zero print()/TODO/debug-logging leakage, all 8 API routers are registered, all 11 Panini CSS modules are imported, the .env.example holds no real secrets, DEBUG defaults to False (gating /api/docs off in prod), and almost every component/util/store is wired. The dead code that remains is well-isolated. The single finding that actually matters for this threat model is the leaderboard "Trend · 7d" sparkline: it renders FABRICATED per-player rank-history data from stubRankTrajectory() for every row, even though a real backend endpoint (GET /leaderboard/snapshots/{user_id}) was purpose-built to feed exactly this column — for a real-money friend pool, showing invented trend lines as if they were real is misleading. The rest are tidy-up items: 7 fully-dead stub functions (kept alive only by their own tests), two dead util modules (flags.ts 243 lines, predictionResult.ts), an orphaned 277-line dashboard widget (DwPredictionNudge), two unused declared dependencies (svelte-motion, psycopg2-binary), two purpose-built-but-never-called backend endpoints, and a couple of stale TODOs. A force-lock dev script (test_match_detail.py) is worth flagging because of WHAT it does (shifts kickoffs into the past) even though it is not wired into runtime.
strengths: Backend app/ is free of print() statements, TODO/FIXME/HACK markers, and debug logging — grep across backend/app returned zero hits for each.
DEBUG defaults to False in config.py (line 20) and DEBUG=${DEBUG:-false} in docker-compose, so the FastAPI /api/docs and /api/redoc endpoints are correctly gated off in production (main.py:36-37).
.env.example is a clean template with placeholder values only (JWT_SECRET_KEY=your-super-secret-key-change-in-production) — no real secrets committed.
All 8 API routers are registered in app/api/__init__.py and all 11 Panini CSS modules are imported in app.css in the documented load-bearing order — no orphaned routers or stylesheets.
Seed/test/calibration scripts under backend/scripts are NOT wired into any runtime path (not referenced by app/, docker-compose command, Makefile, or Dockerfile CMD) — they are manual tooling only.
Frontend console usage is disciplined: the only console call is a dev-gated console.debug('[panini:stub]') in the stub module; production builds emit nothing.

The dashboard widgets (DashGroupStage, DashboardKO, DashboardBetween, DashboardPost) correctly consume real APIs (getMyRankTrajectory, getBracketExposure) rather than stubs — the stub-to-real migration was genuinely completed for the dashboard surface.

dimensionSummaries.title: User Flow & UX Journey (from the code)
dimensionSummaries.summary: The core journey (landing -> login/register/magic-link/Google -> phase-aware dashboard -> predictions wizard -> save/lock feedback -> leaderboard -> results -> rules) is genuinely well-built and unusually polished for a hobby app: blind-pool is enforced server-side (not just hidden in UI), lock state comes from a server-authoritative is_locked flag, drafts persist to localStorage with a restoration banner, the save state-machine gives proper post-confirmation feedback, empty/loading states exist on nearly every list, and there are dedicated phase-specific dashboards with real countdowns. The most serious UX defect is mobile-only: the bottom nav has no Profile or Logout entry, and the only place those live (the desktop masthead avatar menu) is display:none below 700px, so a mobile user (the primary audience) is stranded with no way to sign out, change password, or see their stats. The second-tier issues are flow robustness: there is no global 401 handler, so an expired/invalidated token never bounces the user to login and instead surfaces raw error strings while leaving the dead token in localStorage; and several user-facing copy/number mismatches (48 vs 72 group matches, the documented 5-minute lock vs the actual 15-minute config) will confuse careful readers. None of these let a friend cheat or snoop — the integrity-critical invariants hold — but the mobile dead-end and the 401 handling are real launch-blockers for UX.
strengths: Blind pool holds server-side: /matches/{id}/community returns nothing unless the fixture is locked or finished (backend/app/api/predictions.py:585-586), and the write path re-checks lock with server time (predictions.py:219-222) — the UI gating on the Results page is defense-in-depth, not the only guard.

Lock state is server-authoritative: matchState() keys off fixture.is_locked / fixture.status from the API (matchBreakdown.ts:100-105) rather than the client clock, so a tampered local time can't unlock a card.

Save feedback is honest: the wizard's save button only shows the success/✓ state after Promise.all over the real API calls resolves (predictions/+page.svelte:582-585), matching the 'feedback only after backend confirms' rule.

Strong draft-safety UX: localStorage draft mirror + restoration banner + beforeNavigate/beforeunload guards mean a friend who refreshes mid-entry doesn't silently lose picks (predictions/+page.svelte:533-542, 753-778).

Empty/loading states are present almost everywhere a list can be empty: 'No standings yet' (leaderboard), 'No matches for the current filter', 'No knockout fixtures yet' (predictions), 'Loading stats…' with a Retry button (profile), and a real 'blind until lock' placeholder on the match-detail page.

Auth dead-ends are handled thoughtfully: magic-link with no token redirects to the request form instead of erroring (auth/magic/+page.svelte:15-20), and the OAuth callback shows an explicit failure card with a 'Back to Sign In' button.

Phase taxonomy is well-modelled: deriveUxPhase() composes lock signals into five distinct dashboards with phase-appropriate copy and CTAs, so the landing page is never generic regardless of where the tournament is.

confirmed.title: Weak, committed default JWT secret with no startup validation
confirmed.description: jwt_secret_key is a plain required string with no minimum-length or non-default validation. docker-compose.yml ships `JWT_SECRET_KEY=${JWT_SECRET_KEY:-super-secret-dev-key-change-in-prod}` and .env.example ships `JWT_SECRET_KEY=your-super-secret-key-change-in-production`. If the operator deploys without overriding the env var (easy to forget for a self-hosted hobby app), the HS256 signing key is a publicly known string from this repo. Any tech-savvy friend who recognizes the stack can forge a token with `{"sub": <any user id>, "exp": <future>}` signed with that key and become any user — including minting an is_admin session by pointing sub at an admin user id. This directly enables impersonation, score/paid/admin tampering, and reading everyone's predictions. Because the app boots fine with the weak key, there is no signal that anything is wrong.
confirmed.evidence: config.py:28 `jwt_secret_key: str` (no validation); docker-compose `JWT_SECRET_KEY=${JWT_SECRET_KEY:-super-secret-dev-key-change-in-prod}`; dependencies.py:50 `jwt.encode(to_encode, settings.jwt_secret_key, algorithm=settings.jwt_algorithm)`
confirmed.recommendation: Add a pydantic field_validator on jwt_secret_key that rejects the known default strings and enforces a minimum length (e.g. >=32 chars / 256 bits), failing app startup if violated. Remove the convenient fallback default in docker-compose.yml (require the var to be set), and in .env.example replace the placeholder with an explicit `# generate: python -c "import secrets;print(secrets.token_urlsafe(48))"` instruction rather than a usable-looking value.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL. I located and read every cited site (note: dependencies.py is at backend/app/dependencies.py, not api/dependencies.py — the only inaccuracy in the report, immaterial).

Evidence verified verbatim:
- backend/app/config.py:28 `jwt_secret_key: str` — plain required field, no constraints. A grep for field_validator/model_validator across all of backend/ returns ZERO matches, so there is genuinely no validation anywhere.
- docker-compose.yml:28 `- JWT_SECRET_KEY=${JWT_SECRET_KEY:-super-secret-dev-key-change-in-prod}` — exactly as cited. Critically, this is the SAME compose file used for production: the nginx + cloudflared services are gated behind `profiles: [prod]` within it, so `docker-compose --profile prod up` without JWT_SECRET_KEY set silently uses the committed default.
- backend/.env.example: `JWT_SECRET_KEY=your-super-secret-key-change-in-production` — confirmed.

confirmed.title: JWT_SECRET_KEY falls back to a public hardcoded default — admin-token forgery if prod .env omits it

confirmed.recommendation: Remove the `:-super-secret-dev-key-change-in-prod` fallback so a missing JWT_SECRET_KEY fails the container at startup (config.py already declares jwt_secret_key with no default — let that hard-fail propagate). 2) The prod stack uses this same compose file: Makefile:149 `deploy:` runs `ssh ... 'cd $(REMOTE_PATH) && git pull && docker compose --profile prod up -d --build'`. There is no separate prod compose override; the prod-profile nginx/cloudflared services live in the same docker-compose.yml. So the fallback is in force in production.

3) config.py:28 declares `jwt_secret_key: str` with no Python default — it was clearly designed to hard-fail (pydantic ValidationError) when the env var is missing. But the compose `:-` fallback always supplies a value, so config.py never hard-fails; it silently receives the public literal. The intended fail-closed behavior is defeated by the compose default.

4) HS256 (config.py:29) is symmetric. dependencies.py:50 signs and :69 verifies tokens with settings.jwt_secret_key. Anyone who knows the key (it is in the repo) can forge a token.

5) Token forgery → full compromise: get_current_user (dependencies.py:69-85) only checks the `sub` claim maps to an existing user and is_active; get_admin_user (102-111) checks user.is_admin. Severity assessment: This is a conditional misconfiguration (it only bites if the operator omits/blanks/typos JWT_SECRET_KEY in prod .env), not an unconditional exploitable bug — that is the one honest caveat. However: the default is a publicly-committed, well-known literal; a single missing line is a highly plausible operator slip; the secure-by-default principle (config.py was meant to hard-fail) is actively defeated by the compose fallback; and the impact under the stated threat model (tech-savvy friends, repo access) is total — admin-token forgery defeating every server-side control (blind pool, scoring, paid, admin). The recommendation (drop the fallback so a missing key hard-fails, generate a real secret, optional weak-value assertion when DEBUG is false) is correct and proportionate. Critical is the right severity.

confirmed.title: /predictions/agreements leaks pre-lock predictions (blind-pool violation via iterative probing)
confirmed.description: The /agreements endpoint returns agrees_exact, agrees_outcome and total for every fixture the caller has predicted, computed across ALL predictions for that fixture — with NO check that the fixture is locked or finished (unlike get_community_predictions and get_user_predictions, which both gate on get_fixture_lock_view). The docstring claims 'revealing them pre-lock cannot leak any other user's prediction', but that is false. The counts are relative to the CALLER's own pick, and the caller can change their own pick and re-query. By sweeping their own home/away score across the plausible range and observing how agrees_exact moves, a technical friend reconstructs the exact-score distribution of all other players before the match locks. Boundary cases leak immediately: if agrees_exact == total, every other player picked the caller's exact score; if agrees_outcome == total, everyone picked the same 1/X/2. With ~30 friends and a small score space this fully defeats the server-side blind pool — the hardest invariant in the threat model.
confirmed.evidence: user_preds_q = select(MatchPrediction).where(MatchPrediction.user_id == current_user.id) ... all_preds_result = await session.execute(select(MatchPrediction).where(MatchPrediction.fixture_id.in_(relevant_ids)))  # no lock/finished filter; exact = sum(1 for p in preds if p.home_score == my_h and p.away_score == my_a)
confirmed.recommendation: Gate /agreements the same way community/profile endpoints are: for each requested fixture, compute get_fixture_lock_view (and/or status == FINISHED) and only include fixtures that are locked or finished; drop unlocked fixtures from the response. The DwAgreement widget should only need agreement data for matches that have already locked anyway.

Gating asymmetry is real: get_community_predictions (line 585-590) calls get_fixture_lock_view and returns 403 when `not locked and status != FINISHED`. get_agreements never calls get_fixture_lock_view (the import at line 38 is used at 169/285/385/585 only). Iterative-probing attack is viable: the write paths update_match_prediction and batch_update_predictions (lines 326-327, 344-359) let the caller freely overwrite their own pick while a fixture is unlocked. Because agrees_exact is defined relative to the caller's controllable (my_h,my_a), a technical friend sweeps their own pick across the small score space (CLAUDE.md caps scores at 15/side; realistically 0-5) and re-queries, reconstructing the full exact-score histogram of all ~30 players before lock. agrees_outcome leaks the 1/X/2 split the same way. Refutations attempted and all failed: (1) 'UI only shows agreements on finished matches' — irrelevant, the endpoint is directly callable with any authenticated bearer token, independent of the SvelteKit page that happens to gate display; (2) no additional per-fixture authorization exists, it only requires CurrentUser; (3) predictions ARE editable pre-lock, enabling the sweep; (4) it does NOT restrict to only the caller's own data — it aggregates everyone's predictions.

Threat-model fit is exact: the adversary is an authenticated, somewhat-technical, competitive friend; the violated invariant (server-side blind pool) is the hardest one in scope. Severity nuance: the leak is distributional counts rather than named picks, and pre-lock only — but it fully defeats the server-side blind pool and directly enables cheating (a player can see the whole field's score distribution before committing). Critical is appropriate. Recommendation stands: gate each requested fixture on get_fixture_lock_view / FINISHED and drop unlocked fixtures, exactly like the community endpoint.

confirmed.title: get_user_predictions returns another user's full bracket picks with no lock gate

confirmed.title: Open self-registration auto-joins the live prediction pool (no invite/approval gate)

2. The token is genuinely long-lived: config.py:30 sets `jwt_access_token_expire_minutes: int = 60 * 24 * 7  # 7 days`, and auth.py:187 issues the OAuth token with exactly that expiry. So a 7-day bearer token rides in the URL.

3. frontend/src/routes/auth/callback/+page.svelte:10-17 reads `$page.url.searchParams.get('token')`, calls `handleOAuthCallback(token)`, then `goto('/')`. handleOAuthCallback (auth.ts:113-116) does `token.set(accessToken)`, which persists to localStorage (auth.ts:24, key `predictor_token`). The callback does NOT replaceState or scrub the token from the URL/history, so the query-string entry persists in browser history. Since the token's real home is localStorage, the URL delivery is pure downside, exactly as the finding states.

4. The log-capture concern is concretely real for THIS repo: nginx/nginx.conf:10-14 defines log_format `main` using `"$request"` (the full request line, including query string) and enables `access_log`. A successful login therefore writes `GET /auth/callback?token=<7-day-JWT> HTTP/1.1` verbatim into /var/log/nginx/access.log on the self-hosted VPS.

Refutation attempts fail: Cloudflare does not mitigate leakage into the operator's own nginx logs, Cloudflare's logs, or browser history; there is no URL scrub; and the token is 7 days, not short-lived. Anyone who reads such a log line (or a shared-device's history) can replay the token as a bearer for a week.

Severity calibration to the threat model (tech-savvy friends, ~30 users, self-hosted behind CF): this is a real, established token-handling anti-pattern with multiple confirmed leak channels in this exact codebase, but exploitation requires access to logs or to the victim's browser history/address bar rather than a remote network exploit. That places it at medium — not high (no trivial remote exploit; the average friend can't read VPS logs) and not low (the 7-day lifetime + localStorage destination make the URL exposure gratuitous, and shared-device history / shoulder-surfing are plausible takeover paths in a friend group). Medium as reported is correct.

confirmed.title: Prediction tables have no DB-level uniqueness; Config.unique_together is a SQLModel no-op (duplicate rows / integrity risk)
confirmed.description: MatchPrediction declares `class Config: unique_together = [('user_id','fixture_id')]` and TeamPrediction declares `('user_id','team','stage')`, but SQLModel/SQLAlchemy does NOT honor a `unique_together` Config key — it is silently ignored. The initial migration confirms only NON-unique indexes were created on user_id and fixture_id (lines 95-96, 127-128); there is no UniqueConstraint. The write paths rely on a SELECT-then-INSERT upsert pattern (predictions.py:226-268 single, 336-368 batch). Two concurrent requests for the same (user, fixture) — e.g. the wizard firing /matches/batch twice on a double-tap, or a single + batch save racing — can both miss the existing row and INSERT two MatchPredictions for the same fixture. Duplicates then inflate roster/admin prediction counts and, more importantly, can be double-counted by scoring/leaderboard aggregation, threatening invariant #5 (100% prediction/score integrity). The bracket rewrite's delete-then-insert (predictions.py:527-556) is similarly unprotected against interleaving.
confirmed.evidence: class Config:\n    unique_together = [("user_id", "fixture_id")]   # prediction.py:46-49 — ignored by SQLModel; migration creates only unique=False indexes (initial_schema.py:95-96)
confirmed.recommendation: Add real DB unique constraints via a migration: UNIQUE(user_id, fixture_id) on match_predictions and UNIQUE(user_id, team, stage, phase) on team_predictions (include phase so Phase 1 and Phase 2 rows can coexist). Replace the Config.unique_together with `__table_args__ = (UniqueConstraint(...),)`. Then either use an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT upsert or catch IntegrityError and retry, so concurrent saves converge to one row instead of duplicating.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
CONFIRMED on every point after independently reading the code.

1) No-op Config (prediction.py:46-49, 82-85): MatchPrediction and TeamPrediction declare `class Config: unique_together = [...]`. This is not a SQLAlchemy/SQLModel table-constraint mechanism and is silently ignored. Decisive corroboration: EVERY other model that needs a composite unique uses the correct idiom `__table_args__ = (UniqueConstraint(...),)` — bonus.py:34-35, fifa_ranking.py:14-15, email_send.py:33-34, leaderboard_snapshot.py:30-31, each backed by a real sa.UniqueConstraint in its migration. The two prediction models are the lone outliers using the broken pattern, so this is a genuine slip, not an intentional design.

2) Migration (initial_schema.py:95-96, 127-128): only non-unique indexes (unique=False) on match_predictions(user_id),(fixture_id) and team_predictions(team),(user_id). No UniqueConstraint exists, so the DB will accept duplicate (user_id, fixture_id) rows.

3) Race window confirmed at all layers. Write paths are SELECT-then-INSERT: single update predictions.py:226-268 and batch predictions.py:336-368 both do scalar_one_or_none() then UPDATE-or-INSERT with no DB constraint. database.py creates plain asyncpg sessions; grep found NO isolation_level / SERIALIZABLE / advisory-lock / ON CONFLICT anywhere, so the effective isolation is PostgreSQL default READ COMMITTED — which does not prevent two concurrent txns both seeing "no row" and both inserting. The bracket rewrite (delete-then-insert, predictions.py:527-556) is likewise unprotected against interleaving.

4) Impact on invariant #5 (100% integrity) confirmed. Scoring (scoring.py:600-645) iterates per-row with total_predictions += 1 and per-row point addition; leaderboard.py:64-91 increments correct_outcomes/exact_scores per row; admin.py:124,148,192 uses func.count(MatchPrediction.id). All would over-count a duplicated fixture. Worse, the read path's scalar_one_or_none() (predictions.py:232) would RAISE MultipleResultsFound once a duplicate exists, breaking the user's own subsequent saves — a real, user-visible integrity fault.

Refutation failed: there is no DB constraint, no elevated isolation, no app-level lock, no upsert to converge concurrent writes.

Severity = medium (agreeing with the auditor). It is a real breach of a hard stated invariant with a cheap, already-patterned fix (add UniqueConstraint via migration + ON CONFLICT or IntegrityError-catch). I do NOT raise to high because exploitation needs a true concurrency race rather than a deterministic primitive, the dominant trigger is accidental double-tap, and a scripted cheat is fragile (only helps if the duplicated pick is correct, and risks self-inflicted MultipleResultsFound). Not low because it directly endangers the 100% prediction/score integrity requirement and can corrupt the leaderboard. Recommendation: UNIQUE(user_id, fixture_id) on match_predictions and UNIQUE(user_id, team, stage, phase) on team_predictions (include phase so Phase 1/Phase 2 rows coexist), replace the no-op Config with __table_args__, and use ON CONFLICT / IntegrityError-retry on the write paths.

confirmed.title: POST /leaderboard/invalidate has no authentication dependency
confirmed.description: invalidate_leaderboard_cache takes no CurrentUser/AdminUser dependency at all — it is a state-changing POST callable by anyone, including fully unauthenticated callers (the only thing in front is Cloudflare and CORS, neither of which authenticates). The leaderboard uses a 30s cache (calculate_leaderboard); repeatedly hammering /api/leaderboard/invalidate forces full recomputation on every subsequent fetch, a cheap way for a curious friend to degrade performance during a live match when everyone is polling. It cannot corrupt data, but it is an unauthenticated mutation that bypasses the otherwise-consistent auth model and should not be exposed. (Note: admin score updates already invalidate the cache internally, so this public endpoint is redundant.)
confirmed.evidence: @router.post("/invalidate")\nasync def invalidate_leaderboard_cache() -> dict[str, str]:  # leaderboard.py:120-121 — no Depends, no user, no admin
confirmed.recommendation: Gate it behind AdminUser (consistent with the rest of admin ops), or remove it entirely since scores.py:update_score and admin bonus/answer writes already call invalidate_cache() internally. At minimum require CurrentUser.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL after attempting to refute on every axis.

Code matches the citation verbatim. backend/app/api/leaderboard.py:120-127 defines `@router.post("/invalidate") async def invalidate_leaderboard_cache() -> dict[str, str]` with NO parameters — no Depends, no CurrentUser/AdminUser/OptionalUser. Every other endpoint in this same file takes an explicit auth dependency (get_leaderboard uses OptionalUser; snapshots/climbers/highlights use CurrentUser), making this the lone unauthenticated mutation, so the "bypasses the otherwise-consistent auth model" claim is accurate.

Mount path verified: api/__init__.py:14 includes the router under prefix "/leaderboard", and main.py:50 mounts api_router under settings.api_prefix ("/api"), so the public path is exactly POST /api/leaderboard/invalidate as stated.

No mitigating control exists. The only middleware in main.py (lines 40-47) is CORSMiddleware, which does not authenticate — it governs browser cross-origin policy only and is trivially bypassed by curl/scripts. There is no router-level dependency. So the endpoint is genuinely callable by a fully unauthenticated caller.

State-change + amplification claim verified. services/leaderboard.py:196-202 `invalidate_cache()` resets `_cache = {}`; the cache is a 30s in-memory TTL cache (line 42) and recomputation (lines 132-186) loops over all active users calling calculate_user_points + get_user_match_stats per user. So hammering /invalidate does force repeated full recomputes for everyone polling during a live match. Real, but bounded: in-memory only, ~30 users, self-healing (cache repopulates on the next fetch), and equivalent to the already-available authenticated refresh=true path — so the DoS impact is limited.

Redundancy claim verified: invalidate_cache() is already called internally from scores.py:148 (update_score), admin.py:622, and score_sync.py:121. The public endpoint serves no purpose the system needs.

Could not refute. It does NOT corrupt predictions/scores, leak blind-pool data, or enable cheating — so it is not high/critical. But it is a legitimate unauthenticated, redundant, state-changing endpoint that breaks the app's uniform auth model and offers a cheap perf-degradation lever to a technical friend. Medium (lower end) is the correct severity; recommendation to gate behind AdminUser or delete it is sound.

confirmed.title: Backend runs uvicorn --reload with live source bind-mounts under the prod profile
confirmed.description: The `backend` service has no `profiles:` key, so it is part of every profile including `prod`, and its command is `uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload`. Combined with the source bind-mounts (./backend/app:/app/app, etc., lines 45-57), the production deployment runs the development auto-reload server against host-mounted, mutable source. --reload spawns a file-watcher/reloader process unsuitable for production (higher memory, no graceful worker management, watchdog overhead), and the bind-mounts mean the running prod code is whatever is on the VPS disk rather than the immutable built image — defeating reproducible deploys and letting a host-side file change silently alter scoring/locking logic without a rebuild. For an app with a 100%-integrity requirement this is a real reliability/integrity risk at launch.
confirmed.evidence:
docker-compose.yml:63  command: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload
docker-compose.yml:45-51  volumes: - ./backend/app:/app/app ... - ./config:/app/config
Makefile:149  ssh $(VPS_HOST) '... docker compose --profile prod up -d --build'
confirmed.recommendation: Split prod from dev: either a docker-compose.prod.yml override that drops --reload (the Dockerfile CMD already runs uvicorn without --reload — let prod use the image CMD) and removes the source bind-mounts, or move --reload + mounts into a dev-only service. Run multiple workers via `uvicorn --workers N` or gunicorn+uvicorn workers in prod.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Every factual claim in the finding is verified against the actual code; I could not refute the substance.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
- docker-compose.yml:63 — `command: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload`. This overrides the Dockerfile CMD (backend/Dockerfile:22 = `["uvicorn","app.main:app","--host","0.0.0.0","--port","8000"]`, no --reload).
- The `backend` service (lines 20-63) has NO `profiles:` key, while nginx (112-113) and cloudflared (125-126) do declare `profiles: - prod`. In Compose, a service without `profiles:` is included in EVERY profile invocation, so `--profile prod` starts this exact `backend` definition with `--reload`.
- Source bind-mounts confirmed (lines 45-57): `./backend/app:/app/app`, `./backend/alembic:/app/alembic`, and `./config:/app/config` (the latter holds worldcup2026.yml — the scoring/lock config). So the running prod code/config is host-disk mutable, not the immutable image.
- Makefile:149 deploy = `ssh ... 'cd ~/predictorv2 && git pull && docker compose --profile prod up -d --build'`. The --build rebuilds the image, but because app/config/alembic are bind-mounted, the build artifact is shadowed at runtime — reproducible-deploy guarantee is defeated.
- There is only one docker-compose.yml (no .prod.yml/override exists), so nothing re-drops --reload for prod.
- docs/superpowers/panini-redesign-decisions.md:163 ("the live backend container is bind-mounted to") and :173 ("Backend's --reload picks up the model + endpoint changes automatically once the file edits land") independently confirm this bind-mount + --reload IS the intended, exercised production deploy mechanism on the VPS — not a theoretical leftover.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS THAT FAILED: No prod override file removes --reload; --reload is not gated by DEBUG (config.py:20 debug defaults False; main.py:36 only docs_url keys off debug, and it correctly defaults off — so --reload does NOT leak Swagger/secrets). The bind-mount/reload is real and active in prod.

WHY I LOWERED HIGH→MEDIUM: Under the stated threat model the primary adversary is an authenticated, somewhat-technical FRIEND interacting through the web app. --reload + bind-mounts are not reachable or exploitable by such a user — triggering a silent code/scoring swap or a mid-request reload requires host/SSH access, which a friend does not have. So this is not a cheating/snooping/IDOR attack surface. It IS a genuine data-integrity/reliability weakness (a host-side edit to scoring.py/locking.py or worldcup2026.yml hot-reloads into the live prediction app with no rebuild/version pin, and an in-flight reload can interrupt writes — directly in tension with the 100%-integrity invariant and lock-timing correctness), and the deploy path actively depends on it. That keeps it a real, worth-fixing issue, but for a 30-person hobby app behind Cloudflare with no attacker-reachable vector, medium is the accurate severity rather than high. The recommendation (prod override dropping --reload + bind-mounts, let the image CMD run; optionally --workers/gunicorn) is sound and proportionate.

confirmed.title: No per-account brute-force throttle on email/password login or magic-link verify
confirmed.description: POST /api/auth/login (auth.py:80) does an unrate-limited bcrypt password check per request, and POST /api/auth/magic-link/verify (auth.py:293) does an unrate-limited token guess. The only throttles are nginx's coarse per-IP 10 r/s burst=20 (nginx.conf:40-61) and the 3-per-15min cap on *sending* magic-link emails (magic_link.py:52) — neither limits verify/login attempts per account. A determined friend can attempt thousands of password guesses against a known friend's email (or grind magic-link tokens) within the per-IP budget over time, especially since they share a NAT/Cloudflare path. The magic-link token space (256-bit) makes verify-grinding impractical, but weak human-chosen passwords on the /login path are the real exposure given the threat model is account takeover by a peer.
confirmed.evidence:
auth.py:92  if not verify_password(credentials.password, user.password_hash):  # no attempt counter
auth.py:293  async def verify_magic_link_endpoint(...)  # no per-token/per-IP attempt cap
nginx.conf:40  limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=api:10m rate=10r/s;  # only per-IP, coarse
confirmed.recommendation: Add a lightweight per-account failed-attempt counter (e.g. exponential backoff / temporary lock after N failures within a window) on /login, reusing the DB pattern already built for magic-link rate limiting. This is proportionate (no infra needed) and directly closes the peer-takeover vector. Magic-link verify could similarly cap repeated invalid-token POSTs per IP.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
I independently confirmed every cited fact. /api/auth/login (backend/app/api/auth.py:80-109) looks up the user by email and calls verify_password (bcrypt.checkpw, dependencies.py:25-31) with NO failed-attempt counter, lockout, or backoff. /api/auth/magic-link/verify (auth.py:293-330) calls verify_magic_link with no per-token/per-IP attempt cap. The User model (models/user.py:24-49) has no failed_login/locked_until fields. The ONLY throttles are nginx's per-IP limit_req zone=api rate=10r/s burst=20 (nginx/nginx.conf:40,61) and the 3-per-15min cap that gates magic-link SENDING (magic_link.py:52,244-250) — neither limits verify/login attempts per account. main.py adds only CORSMiddleware, no rate-limit middleware. Password floor is min_length=8 (schemas/auth.py:17), so weak human-chosen passwords are permitted, making the /login brute-force surface real.

So the finding accurately describes the code. On severity, I lower it from high to medium for the stated threat model: (1) The magic-link verify-grind is a non-issue — token_urlsafe(32) is 256 bits of entropy, and the finding itself concedes this; verify-grinding is computationally impossible, so only the /login password path is a genuine exposure. (2) The nginx per-IP limit (now correctly keyed on CF-Connecting-IP via set_real_ip_from + real_ip_header, nginx.conf:34-37) does cap a single attacker to ~10 r/s, which is a coarse but real ceiling — online brute force of a weak 8-char password is slow but feasible over days for a determined peer who knows a friend's email and that they reuse a weak password. (3) bcrypt makes each guess CPU-costly server-side, and accounts can also use Google SSO or magic-link (no password), shrinking the vulnerable population to whoever set a weak email/password. This is a real account-takeover-by-peer vector but bounded and partially mitigated, fitting medium rather than high. The recommendation (a lightweight DB-backed per-account failed-attempt counter with temporary lockout, reusing the magic-link rate-limit pattern) is proportionate and appropriate — no enterprise infra needed.

confirmed.title: Leaderboard cold recompute is a nested N+1 explosion (per-user, per-finished-fixture queries)
confirmed.description: calculate_leaderboard loops over every active user and calls calculate_user_points + get_user_match_stats for each. calculate_user_points then issues one get_outcome_counts() query PER finished fixture (scoring.py:620 inside the row loop), plus get_actual_advancement (a full knockout-fixtures scan, scoring.py:654) recomputed for every user, plus calculate_group_position_bonus and calculate_bonus_points. With the 104-match group stage fully scored and ~30 users, a single cold build is on the order of 30 * (104 outcome-count queries + ~10 standings/advancement/bonus queries) ≈ several thousand SQL round-trips. The 30s TTL hides this in steady state, but every cache miss (TTL expiry, or any invalidate_cache() call after a score/bonus update) pays the full cost, and the same calculate_user_points/calculate_leaderboard is reused by profile, trajectory, highlights, and snapshots. At 30 users this is survivable but slow (likely multi-second) on a small VPS, and it scales as O(users * fixtures).
confirmed.evidence: leaderboard.py:138 `for user in users:` -> scoring.py:611 `for prediction, score, fixture in rows:` -> scoring.py:620 `outcome_counts = await get_outcome_counts(session, fixture.id)` (one query per finished fixture, per user). get_actual_advancement (scoring.py:654) and the whole standings chain are recomputed identically for every user.
confirmed.recommendation: Hoist the per-fixture and tournament-global computations out of the per-user loop: compute outcome_counts for all fixtures once (single GROUP BY fixture_id, outcome query), compute get_actual_advancement and actual group standings once per build, and pass them into a refactored calculate_user_points. Replace the per-fixture get_outcome_counts call with a dict lookup. This collapses the cold build from O(users*fixtures) queries to a small constant number of queries.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL but severity overstated. The cited code does exactly what the finding claims:

- backend/app/services/leaderboard.py:138-140 — `for user in users:` loops over every active user, calling `calculate_user_points` AND `get_user_match_stats` for each. `get_user_match_stats` (leaderboard.py:64) is itself a redundant full per-user query that re-fetches the same finished-match prediction set `calculate_user_points` already pulls.
- scoring.py:611,620 — confirmed: inside `for prediction, score, fixture in rows:`, `await get_outcome_counts(session, fixture.id)` issues one SELECT per finished fixture, per user. With the 104-match group stage scored × 30 users that is ~3,120 outcome-count round-trips alone.
- scoring.py:654 — `get_actual_advancement(session)` is a full knockout-fixtures-with-scores scan recomputed identically for every user (30× redundant), and lines 419-424 in `calculate_group_position_bonus` add three more standings queries per user, plus `calculate_bonus_points` per user. The O(users × fixtures) characterization is accurate.
- Reuse confirmed across snapshots.py:46, profile.py:20-23, trajectory (leaderboard.py:154), highlights (leaderboard.py:548), scores.py:45. Cost is further multiplied by per-phase cache keys (overall/phase_1/phase_2 → up to 3 full builds for a dashboard).

The recommendation (hoist `get_actual_advancement`/standings out of the loop, batch all outcome counts into a single GROUP BY fixture_id query, drop redundant `get_user_match_stats`) is correct and worthwhile.

WHY MEDIUM, NOT HIGH:
1. This is a pure performance/scalability concern — it does NOT enable cheating, snooping, IDOR, prediction/lock tampering, or data corruption, which are the explicitly-prioritized threats for this audit. The audit instructions say to keep severity proportionate to a 30-person hobby app and not pad with overkill.
2. Existing mitigations meaningfully reduce real-world impact: `fixture_id`/`user_id` are indexed (prediction.py:30-31), so each query is a cheap index lookup; the 30s in-memory TTL hides steady-state cost; and `take_daily_snapshots` is idempotent (effectively once/day). The finding itself concedes "At 30 users this is survivable."
3. No DoS vector — callers are authenticated friends; even `?refresh=true` triggers one serial build per request.

It remains a genuine, fixable inefficiency (thousands of serial awaits per cold build on a tiny VPS could give multi-second leaderboard loads after every score update), so it is not a false positive and is worth addressing — but "high" overstates its importance under this threat model. Adjusted to medium.

confirmed.title: Public leaderboard endpoint allows uncached, on-demand full recompute (cache-stampede / abuse vector)
confirmed.description: GET /leaderboard/ is OptionalUser (effectively public) and accepts refresh=true, which sets force_refresh and bypasses the TTL entirely, forcing the full PERF-1 recompute on every such request. There is no stampede guard: the cache is a plain dict with no lock, so when the TTL expires (or after invalidate_cache during a live match-day score sync) multiple concurrent requests from the 10-20 active users all miss simultaneously and each runs the full recompute in parallel, multiplying DB load at exactly the moment scores are changing. A curious/technical friend hammering ?refresh=true could also pin the single worker. Not a data-integrity or cheating issue, but a genuine availability defect on match day.

confirmed.recommendation: Restrict refresh=true to admins (or remove it; invalidate_cache already covers the legitimate 'scores changed' case). Add a simple per-cache-key asyncio.Lock around the recompute so only one coroutine rebuilds while others await the result (single-flight). Optionally serve slightly-stale cache while a refresh is in flight.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Cited code verified exactly as described. backend/app/api/leaderboard.py:98 declares `refresh: bool = Query(False, ...)` and line 117 passes it straight to `calculate_leaderboard(session, force_refresh=refresh, phase=phase)`. The endpoint's `_user: OptionalUser` (line 97) makes it effectively public. In backend/app/services/leaderboard.py the cache is a bare module dict `_cache: dict[str, CachedLeaderboard]` (line 41) with a 30s TTL (line 42), and the guard at line 117 `if not force_refresh and cache_key in _cache:` has no lock/single-flight — force_refresh skips it entirely.

The recompute is genuinely heavy, corroborating the cache-stampede concern: calculate_leaderboard loops over every active user (leaderboard.py:138) calling calculate_user_points, which itself issues a per-finished-fixture get_outcome_counts query inside a loop (scoring.py:620) plus actual_advancement/group_position/bonus queries — an O(users x fixtures) un-batched async workload. Deployment is a single uvicorn worker (Dockerfile:22, no --workers flag) with no rate limiting anywhere in the backend (no slowapi/limiter). Crucially, invalidate_cache() IS on the live match-day path: score_sync.py:121 calls it after each sync and the scheduler polls every 60s (POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 60.0), so the cache is wiped about once a minute exactly when 10-20 friends are watching/refreshing. So the stampede window the finding describes is real and recurs on match day, and a technical friend can amplify it with ?refresh=true.

I could not refute the existence of the defect; every claim is backed by code. However, I am lowering severity from high to medium. The 30s TTL plus per-invalidation absorption means the stampede window is narrow (the seconds after each 60s invalidate/TTL expiry); with ~10-20 users on a single event loop the worst realistic outcome is leaderboard latency spikes / a briefly-pinned worker, not a hard outage, lost predictions, score tampering, or blind-pool snooping. The finding itself concedes it is not a data-integrity or cheating issue. Under the stated hobby-app/30-friends/Cloudflare threat model, a degraded-but-self-recovering leaderboard is a medium availability concern. The proposed fixes (admin-gate or remove `refresh` since invalidate_cache already covers the legitimate 'scores changed' case, and add a per-cache-key asyncio.Lock for single-flight) are cheap and proportionate, not enterprise overkill.

I tried hard to refute via the strongest angle: "maybe the backend endpoint is itself a non-functional stub, so the finding's 'real endpoint already built' premise is false." It is not — the path is genuinely functional: `take_daily_snapshots()` (services/snapshots.py:37) writes real snapshot rows and is invoked from the score scheduler (services/score_scheduler.py:63); `get_user_trajectory()` reads them; `_build_trajectory()` appends the live rank. The very same trajectory API (`getMyRankTrajectory`) is used FOR REAL by four dashboard widgets (DashGroupStage, DashboardPost, DashboardBetween, DashboardKO). Under the stated threat model this is NOT a security/cheating/snooping/IDOR/data-integrity boundary: no prediction or score is corrupted, no blind-pool leak, no privilege change. Harm is real but bounded: in a stakes-bearing friend pool a competitive/technical player could read false momentum into the sparkline; mitigants are that the line is deterministic per reload and anchored to the true current rank (so it looks plausible) and that dev devtools emit `[panini:stub] rankTrajectory`. Given a real, working endpoint sits unused while fabricated trend lines reach users, medium is the fair rating (defensibly low, but the "fabricated-as-genuine + idle real source" combination keeps it at medium). Recommendation stands: wire the column to getRankTrajectory()/the snapshots endpoint (ideally embed each row's trajectory in the leaderboard payload to avoid N calls), or hide/relabel the column until wired, then delete the stub import.

confirmed.title: No global 401 handler — expired/invalid token never bounces to login and leaves a dead token in localStorage
confirmed.description: ApiClient.request() throws a generic `new Error(error.detail)` for every non-OK response with no special case for 401/403 (client.ts:34-39). There is no interceptor that, on a 401, clears the token and redirects to /login (a repo-wide grep finds zero references to status 401). The only place a bad token is cleared is fetchUser() on initial app load (auth.ts:92-101). So once the app is running, if the JWT expires or is revoked mid-session, every page's onMount fetch (leaderboard polling, predictions save, results) rejects with a raw backend string like 'Could not validate credentials' rendered into the page's error slot, the user stays on a half-broken authenticated-looking screen, and the stale token remains in localStorage (so a refresh re-attempts with the same dead token). For a prediction app this is also a data-integrity footgun: a user could type picks into the wizard, hit Save, get an opaque error, and not realise their session died and nothing was saved. The audit explicitly asks 'expired token -> does a 401 cleanly bounce to login?' — currently it does not.
confirmed.evidence: client.ts:34-39: `if (!response.ok) { const error = await response.json().catch(...); throw new Error(error.detail); }` — no status inspection, no token clear, no redirect. `grep -rn '401' frontend/src` returns only the generic `HTTP ${response.status}` string template.
confirmed.recommendation: In ApiClient.request(), detect `response.status === 401` (and arguably 403 for revoked accounts), clear the token via the auth store and `goto('/login')` before throwing. Easiest decoupled approach: pass an onUnauthorized callback into the ApiClient from auth.ts (the store already imports goto and owns logout()). Show a brief 'Your session expired, please sign in again' message on the login page.

2. `grep -rn '401' frontend/src` returns ZERO hits. There is no interceptor, no `onUnauthorized` callback, no status-based branching anywhere in the frontend API layer. Confirmed.

3. The ONLY place a bad token is cleared is fetchUser() in auth.ts:92-101, which runs on initial app load via initAuth() (auth.ts:119-124). `$isAuthenticated` is derived from `token` (auth.ts:37), and `token` is never set to null on a mid-session non-OK API response. Over a ~2-month tournament with 30 regulars, tokens expire roughly weekly — this is a recurring, predictable event, not an exotic edge case. The worst path is the prediction wizard near a lock deadline: stale tab, type picks, hit Save, get an opaque error, picks not saved, session silently dead.

SEVERITY CALIBRATION (lowered from high to medium): Under the stated threat model this is genuinely a data-integrity/session-hygiene UX footgun, which the threat model does care about. BUT it does NOT enable cheating, snooping, IDOR, or silent server-side corruption — the backend correctly rejects the dead token (401), so nothing wrong is persisted, the blind pool and scoring integrity hold server-side, and there is no exploit a friend could weaponize. The realistic harm is a confusing stranded screen, a stale token requiring a refresh/relogin, and a user potentially not realizing a save failed. That is real and worth fixing (the recommended fix — detect 401 in ApiClient.request, clear token via the store, goto('/login') with a brief "session expired" message — is proportionate and cheap), but it falls short of high for a 30-person hobby app with no data-loss-on-server and no security bypass. Medium is the correct rating. The recommendation is sound and not enterprise overkill.

confirmed.title: Google OAuth callback has no CSRF state parameter
confirmed.description: GoogleSSO from fastapi-sso 0.19.0 defaults to requires_state=False, and the code never opts in (get_login_redirect() is called with no state= argument, and there is no SessionMiddleware/cookie to carry one). In verify_and_process (fastapi_sso/sso/base.py), state is only validated when both a state query param AND a matching `sso_state` cookie are present; with neither set, the OAuth callback accepts any well-formed Google authorization code with no cross-request binding. This removes the OAuth login-CSRF protection: an attacker could initiate a flow with their own Google account and trick a victim into completing it (login-CSRF), or more realistically it just means the flow has no replay/forgery binding. For a 30-person app this is lower impact than AUTH-1/2 but is a genuine missing control on the account-auth path. (Note: a fresh GoogleSSO instance is also constructed per request, so even PKCE/state state could not survive across the redirect→callback boundary without external storage.)
confirmed.evidence: auth.py:123 `return await google_sso.get_login_redirect()` (no state); auth.py:139 `google_user = await google_sso.verify_and_process(request)`; fastapi_sso base.py: state checked only `if self._state is not None` and against `request.cookies.get('sso_state')`; GoogleSSO does not set requires_state=True.
confirmed.recommendation: Enable state: add Starlette SessionMiddleware (or pass an explicit state and store it in a short-lived signed cookie), construct the SSO with state support, and pass/verify the state across get_login_redirect and verify_and_process. fastapi-sso documents the per-method `state=` argument for exactly this.

Technically the finding is fully accurate and I could not refute it. Confirmed against the actually-installed fastapi-sso 0.19.0:
- backend/app/api/auth.py:38-46 builds a fresh GoogleSSO per request with no use_state/state config; GoogleSSO (site-packages/fastapi_sso/sso/google.py) does not override requires_state, and SSOBase.requires_state defaults to False (base.py:106).
- auth.py:123 calls get_login_redirect() with no state=. In base.py:338-346, because requires_state is False and state is None, no state is generated and the sso_state cookie is never set (line 344 guards on `if state is not None`).
- auth.py:139 calls verify_and_process(request). In base.py:406-414, self._state is read from the query param but validated only `if sso_state is not None` (the cookie) — which was never set — so the equality check is skipped and no cross-request binding is enforced. The callback therefore accepts any well-formed Google auth code with no CSRF/state binding.
- I verified there is NO SessionMiddleware anywhere; main.py:41 adds only CORSMiddleware. This confirms the auditor's "no cookie to carry state" point, and the per-request instance construction means even generated state couldn't survive the redirect→callback boundary. Every concrete claim checks out.

However, the medium rating overstates impact for THIS app/threat model, so I lower to low:
- The classic damaging login-CSRF variant relies on the callback setting a server-side session/cookie on the requesting browser (session fixation). This app does NOT do that. auth.py:185-192 issues a JWT and redirects to /auth/callback?token=<JWT>; the frontend (routes/auth/callback/+page.svelte) reads the token from the URL into client storage. There is no server session to fix.

In a competitive 30-person prediction pool this is self-defeating and grants the attacker nothing — no read of others' predictions (blind pool intact), no score/paid/admin tampering, no data corruption, no IDOR. It does not enable any of the cheat/snoop/integrity outcomes the threat model prioritizes.
- CORS/Cloudflare do not mitigate it (top-level navigation), so it is a genuine missing control — but a low-likelihood, low-payoff one. It is a legitimate hardening item (enable state via SessionMiddleware or a signed short-lived cookie + state= on both get_login_redirect and verify_and_process), not a launch blocker.

Real, correctly located, no compensating control — but proportionately low, not medium.

confirmed.title: Magic-link request endpoint leaks account existence (enumeration); login path differs in shape
confirmed.description: POST /api/auth/magic-link/request returns HTTP 404 'No account found for this email' for unknown addresses and 403 'Account is inactive' for deactivated ones, versus 200 for known-active accounts. The code comment explicitly waves this off ('enumeration risk is essentially zero' for a friend group), which is a defensible call, but it also reveals which of the ~30 friends have accounts and their active/inactive status to anyone who can hit the endpoint, and it's inconsistent with the intended generic behavior. The /login path correctly returns a uniform 401, so the two auth entry points disagree.

confirmed.recommendation: Make the request endpoint return a uniform 200 'if an account exists, a link has been sent' for unknown/inactive emails (still rate-limited), and only branch internally. If you deliberately keep the friendly 404 for UX, update the misleading docstring and accept the documented tradeoff explicitly. Low effort, removes the inconsistency.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:

The enumeration capability is NOT new — /register already leaks existence with "Email already registered" (auth.py:52-58), so the magic-link endpoint adds no enumeration power the app didn't already have. Members' emails are largely mutually known. There is no cheat/snoop/data-integrity impact: it doesn't let anyone edit predictions, read another user's predictions before lock, alter scores/paid/admin flags, or shift lock timing. The only marginally-sensitive extra signal is active/inactive status (reveals an admin deactivated someone), which is minor. RateLimited (3/15min) already curbs spamming.
