confirmed.title: Google account-linking silently flips an email user to GOOGLE provider, disabling password & lockout
confirmed.description: When a Google callback matches an existing email-based user (linking branch), the code sets user.google_id and overwrites user.auth_provider = AuthProvider.GOOGLE. After this, change_password returns 403 (auth.py:206) because the provider is GOOGLE, even though the user still has a password_hash. More subtly, linking is keyed purely on Google's verified email == stored email with no confirmation step; while Google emails are verified, this means whoever controls a Google account with a friend's email address can claim that friend's existing account (and inherit its predictions/points/admin flag). For this trusted friend-group it's low risk, but the silent provider flip is a correctness surprise and the auto-link removes the password as a recovery path.
confirmed.evidence: auth.py:162-164 `user.google_id = google_user.id; user.auth_provider = AuthProvider.GOOGLE`; change_password 403s on non-EMAIL provider (auth.py:206-210).
confirmed.recommendation: Either keep auth_provider as a non-destructive set of capabilities (allow both password and Google once linked) or, at minimum, don't downgrade password access on link; and consider only auto-linking when the existing account has no password_hash, otherwise require an authenticated link step. At hobby scale, documenting the behavior is acceptable.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL, severity correctly LOW. The cited code is exactly as described. In backend/app/api/auth.py:161-164, the Google-callback linking branch matches an existing user by email and sets user.google_id = google_user.id; user.auth_provider = AuthProvider.GOOGLE, while NOT clearing password_hash (the User model password_hash field, user.py:32, is left intact). Then change_password (auth.py:206-210) raises 403 whenever auth_provider != EMAIL. So a user who originally registered with email+password, after a single Google sign-in that matches their email, is silently flipped to GOOGLE provider and can no longer rotate their password via /me/password even though a valid password_hash still sits in the row. That is a genuine correctness surprise and orphaned-credential bug — confirmed, not a false positive.

Two corrections to the finding's framing that keep this firmly at LOW and not higher: (1) The finding implies the password becomes unusable. It does not — /login (auth.py:80-109) authenticates purely on password_hash + verify_password and never inspects auth_provider, so password LOGIN still works after the flip; only the self-service change_password path is broken. Calling it "disabling password" overstates the impact. (2) The account-claiming sub-concern (someone controlling a Google account with a friend's email inheriting the friend's account/predictions/admin flag) is theoretical and already subsumed by the app's own magic-link login (services/magic_link.py), which grants account access to anyone who controls the target mailbox. Since the OAuth link requires Google to verify that same email, control of the mailbox already yields account access independent of OAuth — the linking branch adds no incremental takeover vector. There is also no password-reset endpoint at all, so "removes the password recovery path" is not a regression from any prior reset flow.

It is purely a UX/correctness wart (silent provider downgrade + an unreachable password_hash). LOW is the right severity. The recommendation (treat auth_provider as additive capabilities, or only auto-link when there is no existing password_hash, or simply allow change_password when a password_hash exists regardless of provider — and at hobby scale, documenting it) is proportionate and sound.

confirmed.title: Stateless 7-day JWT with no server-side revocation; logout is client-only
confirmed.description: Tokens live 7 days (jwt_access_token_expire_minutes = 60*24*7) and there is no token denylist or jti/version check — get_current_user only verifies signature+exp and re-loads the user. logout() merely clears localStorage client-side; a token already copied elsewhere (see AUTH-5 URL leak, or XSS reading localStorage) remains valid for up to a week with no way to revoke short of deactivating the account (is_active=False, which is the only effective kill switch and is admin-only). For a competitive friend pool, a leaked/stolen token is a full-week impersonation window.
confirmed.evidence: config.py:30 `jwt_access_token_expire_minutes: int = 60 * 24 * 7  # 7 days`; auth.ts:104-111 logout only clears stores/localStorage; get_current_user has no revocation check.
confirmed.recommendation: Acceptable for the scale, but tighten: shorten the access-token lifetime (e.g. 24h) and/or add a per-user token_version claim checked in get_current_user so a 'sign out everywhere' / compromise response is possible. Document that admin deactivation is the only current revocation lever.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Every factual claim in AUTH-8 is confirmed by the actual code; I could not refute it.

1. 7-day token lifetime: backend/app/config.py:30 `jwt_access_token_expire_minutes: int = 60 * 24 * 7  # 7 days` — exact match. This value is applied at every token-mint site (auth.py:74, :106, :187, :330).

2. No jti / token_version / denylist: backend/app/dependencies.py:49 `create_access_token` encodes only `to_encode = {"sub": user_id, "exp": expire}` — no jti, no version claim. `get_current_user` (dependencies.py:53-85) calls `jwt.decode` (signature+exp only), re-loads the user by id, and checks just `user.is_active`. There is no revocation/version/denylist check anywhere — a repo-wide grep for token_version/jti/denylist/blacklist/revoke found only magic-link revocation, nothing for access tokens.

3. Logout is client-only: frontend/src/lib/stores/auth.ts:104-111 `logout()` only clears stores/localStorage and navigates to /login; no backend call. There is no backend logout endpoint at all (grep confirms none in app/api/).

4. is_active=False is the only kill switch and is admin-only: the sole mutation site is admin.py:221 `user.is_active = not user.is_active`, inside the admin router. get_current_user enforces it at dependencies.py:82-83.

5. Corroborating leak vector (AUTH-5 cross-ref): the OAuth callback returns the token in a URL query string — auth.py:192 `RedirectResponse(url=f"{frontend_url}/auth/callback?token={access_token}")`, read on the frontend via `$page.url.searchParams.get('token')` (routes/auth/callback/+page.svelte:10) and then persisted to XSS-readable localStorage (auth.ts:13-28,41-44). So a token genuinely can land in history/referrer/logs, and the 7-day stateless design means such a leak yields up to a week of unrevocable impersonation.

For a ~30-person friend pool behind Cloudflare, the realistic scenario is a shared device where a friend lifts another's localStorage token and impersonates them for a week with no self-service revocation. That is a plausible cheating/snooping vector but a mild, bounded one. The finding's own recommendation (shorten lifetime to ~24h and/or add a token_version claim checked in get_current_user; document that admin deactivation is the only current lever) is proportionate and not enterprise-overkill. Confirmed real, correctly rated low.

confirmed.title: Bracket prediction write accepts arbitrary unbounded team/stage strings with no allowlist or list-size cap

confirmed.evidence: predictions.py:534-540: prediction = TeamPrediction(user_id=current_user.id, team=pred_data.team, stage=pred_data.stage, group_position=pred_data.group_position, phase=current_phase)  # no team/stage allowlist, no len(bracket_data.predictions) cap. schema: class TeamAdvancementPrediction: team: str; stage: str  # no max_length, no enum
confirmed.recommendation: Constrain TeamAdvancementPrediction.stage to a Literal/enum of the six valid stages plus 'group', add max_length (~64) to team, and reject teams not present in fetch_competition_teams(session). Cap BracketPredictionUpdate.predictions length (e.g. <= ~120, the realistic bracket size) and add a server-side max_length on the TeamPrediction.team/stage columns. Even worst-case (~12 rows of ~1MB) is trivial storage for a 30-user hobby app's PostgreSQL — not a real storage-abuse or DoS vector.

3. There is NO data-integrity violation (predictions/scores are not corrupted or lost; audit log via record_bonus_prediction_change holds), NO blind-pool/snoop bypass, NO lock-timing or phase-separation impact, and NO injection/XSS (email is escaped; answers are compared via normalized case/accent folding at scoring time, never interpreted as code).

Under the stated threat model (curious technical friend, ~30 users, behind Cloudflare), this is a legitimate but low-impact input-hardening gap. The recommendation (add max_length ~100 to the schema field and column, cap the batch to ~number-of-questions, optionally validate team answers against eligible_teams) is sound and proportionate. Real = true, adjusted severity = low rather than medium.

confirmed.title: Match-prediction batch endpoint has no list-size cap
confirmed.description: batch_update_predictions takes predictions_data: list[MatchPredictionCreate] with no max-items constraint and loops issuing a SELECT (for the fixture) and a SELECT (for the existing prediction) per element, plus per-element flush/refresh and an audit-history insert. An authenticated user can submit an arbitrarily large list, each triggering multiple DB round-trips, as a cheap amplification/DoS lever. Each individual prediction is bounded (le=20) and unknown fixtures are skipped, so this is integrity-neutral but a resource-abuse concern. Proportionate severity for a 30-user app behind Cloudflare: low.
confirmed.evidence: predictions.py:303-313: async def batch_update_predictions(predictions_data: list[MatchPredictionCreate], ...): for pred_data in predictions_data:  # unbounded loop, 2 SELECTs + flush + history insert each
confirmed.recommendation: Cap the batch list to a sane upper bound (e.g. the total fixture count, ~104) via a Field(max_length=...) on a wrapper model or a length check at the top of the handler that 422s oversized payloads.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Code claims verified accurately. backend/app/api/predictions.py:303-308 declares `batch_update_predictions(predictions_data: list[MatchPredictionCreate], ...)` with a bare `list[...]` and no size cap — I grepped for max_length/conlist/Annotated/length checks in both the handler and schemas/prediction.py and found none. The loop (lines 313-403) does, per element: SELECT Fixture (315), SELECT MatchPrediction (336-341), an audit-history insert via record_match_prediction_change (350/371), session.flush() (370 and/or 382), and session.refresh() (383) — matching (and slightly exceeding) the described per-element DB cost. Each element is bounded by MatchPredictionCreate (ge=0, le=20 at schemas/prediction.py:16-17) and unknown/locked fixtures are skipped (318-327), so the finding is correctly characterized as integrity-neutral resource abuse, not a cheating/snooping vector. I keep severity at low rather than refuting because the concern is real but well-mitigated under the stated threat model: nginx rate-limits /api to 10r/s with burst=20 per real client IP (nginx.conf:40,61) behind Cloudflare, so a tech-savvy friend cannot sustain a flood; there is a single commit at line 405 (no per-element commit), which limits amplification; and the blast radius is one slow request against one Postgres session, bounded by request timeout. No integrity, lock-bypass, blind-pool, or privilege impact. The recommended ~104-item cap is a cheap, proportionate hardening fix. Real finding, correctly self-rated low.

confirmed.title: Google OAuth callback reflects raw exception text to the client
confirmed.description: The Google callback wraps verify_and_process in `except Exception as e: raise HTTPException(... detail=f"Failed to authenticate with Google: {e}")`. The underlying exception string (from fastapi-sso / httpx / token exchange) is returned verbatim to the caller, which can leak internal details — token-exchange URLs, partial config, library internals — and is an information-disclosure smell. Similarly the magic-link MagicLinkError handler returns `detail=str(e)` (auth.py:323), though those messages are author-controlled. Not catastrophic for a friend group, but it's needless leakage and can aid a curious user probing the auth flow.
confirmed.evidence:
auth.py:140-144  except Exception as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=f"Failed to authenticate with Google: {e}")
auth.py:323  detail=str(e) or "Login failed",
confirmed.recommendation: Return a generic message to the client ("Google sign-in failed, please try again") and log the exception server-side via logger.exception. Avoid interpolating raw exception strings into HTTP detail.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The cited code exists exactly as reported. backend/app/api/auth.py:140-144 wraps `google_sso.verify_and_process(request)` in `except Exception as e:` and interpolates `{e}` verbatim into the HTTP 400 detail: `detail=f"Failed to authenticate with Google: {e}"`. This is a genuine uncontrolled-exception reflection — the broad `except Exception` catches fastapi-sso / httpx / token-exchange / JSON-parse internals, so library plumbing and network error text can be returned to the caller. So the core claim is REAL.

The second cited line, auth.py:323 (`detail=str(e) or "Login failed"`), is effectively a non-issue: it only handles the base `MagicLinkError`, but grep confirms the base class is never raised directly anywhere (magic_link.py raises only the subclasses TokenInvalid/TokenExpired/TokenAlreadyUsed at lines 304/313/315, all caught by earlier `except` clauses with hardcoded messages). The finding itself concedes these are author-controlled. So that part is essentially dead/benign — I am not counting it toward severity.

Severity assessment under the stated threat model (curious tech-savvy friend, ~30 users, behind Cloudflare): I am lowering medium → low. The leak cannot advance any of the threat-model harms. It does NOT expose the Google client_secret (get_google_sso at auth.py:41-46 uses the secret to perform the token exchange; fastapi-sso surfaces Google's error response / generic library text, not the configured secret). The redirect_uri and client_id are public by OAuth design anyway. Critically, the leaked exception text cannot reveal another user's predictions, scores, paid/admin flags (no IDOR/blind-pool impact), cannot tamper with predictions, and cannot shift lock/kickoff timing — the data-integrity and snooping invariants are untouched. The realistic worst case is a curious friend tampering with the OAuth callback and seeing httpx/token-exchange plumbing strings, which is hygiene noise, not an exploit path. The recommendation (generic client message + server-side logger.exception) is correct and proportionate, so this is worth fixing as a low-severity cleanup rather than a medium.

confirmed.title: No HSTS, Content-Security-Policy, or Referrer-Policy headers
confirmed.description: nginx adds X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, and a (deprecated, harmless) X-XSS-Protection, but no Strict-Transport-Security, Content-Security-Policy, or Referrer-Policy. The app is served over HTTPS via Cloudflare Tunnel, so HSTS would normally be set at the Cloudflare edge rather than this origin nginx (origin only sees HTTP from cloudflared) — verify HSTS is enabled in the Cloudflare dashboard. CSP matters more here: the magic-link JWT arrives via URL and is stored in localStorage (predictor_token), so any XSS would exfiltrate the token; a CSP would harden against that. Referrer-Policy matters because the OAuth callback redirects to /auth/callback?token=... — without a restrictive referrer policy the token-bearing URL can leak via Referer to any external resource the callback page loads.
confirmed.evidence:
nginx.conf:55-57  add_header X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN" always;  add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff" always;  add_header X-XSS-Protection "1; mode=block" always;  # no HSTS/CSP/Referrer-Policy
auth.py:192  return RedirectResponse(url=f"{frontend_url}/auth/callback?token={access_token}")
confirmed.recommendation: Add `add_header Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" always;` and a starter Content-Security-Policy at the nginx server block, and confirm HSTS is enabled at the Cloudflare edge (or add it here if cloudflared forwards as https). Consider moving the OAuth token out of the query string (e.g. fragment or short-lived exchange code) as a follow-up to avoid token-in-URL leakage.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL, but I lowered severity from medium to low.

Factual claims all confirmed:
- nginx/nginx.conf:55-57 adds X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, X-XSS-Protection and nothing else. No Strict-Transport-Security, Content-Security-Policy, or Referrer-Policy appear anywhere in the file (grep + full read confirm).
- backend/app/api/auth.py:192 confirmed verbatim: `return RedirectResponse(url=f"{frontend_url}/auth/callback?token={access_token}")` — OAuth JWT is delivered in the URL query string.
- Token is persisted to localStorage under key 'predictor_token' (frontend/src/lib/stores/auth.ts:13,24), so any XSS can read it directly. Token TTL is 7 days (config.py:30: 60*24*7), so a leaked token is long-lived = effective account takeover.
- Deployment topology matches the finding's nuance: nginx listens only on port 80 / HTTP (nginx.conf:51) behind a cloudflared sidecar (docker-compose.yml:116). The finding correctly reframes HSTS as an edge concern rather than naively demanding it on the HTTP-only origin — this is accurate, not over-reach.

I independently strengthened the Referrer-Policy angle: app.html (the global shell, rendered on EVERY page including /auth/callback) loads stylesheets from fonts.googleapis.com and fonts.gstatic.com. So there genuinely are cross-origin subresource requests on the token-bearing callback page. The callback handler (auth/callback/+page.svelte:9-22 -> handleOAuthCallback) consumes the token then goto('/') but never strips ?token= from the URL/history, and nginx's log_format (lines 10-12) logs $request including the query string — so the token does persist in browser history and access logs.

Why I downgraded to low rather than medium:
- The single most impactful header (HSTS) is correctly handled at the Cloudflare edge, not a bug on this origin nginx. That part is informational.
- Modern browser default referrer policy is strict-origin-when-cross-origin, which already strips the path+query from cross-origin Referer headers — so the Google Fonts requests do NOT leak the ?token= value by default in current browsers. The explicit Referrer-Policy header is belt-and-suspenders, not a fix for an active leak.
- CSP is pure defense-in-depth: no XSS sink was demonstrated, and with only ~30 trusted friends behind a private Cloudflare tunnel (noindex/nofollow), the realistic XSS exposure is low. The recommendations are proportionate (two header lines + an edge config check + a sensible token-out-of-URL follow-up), not enterprise padding.

Net: the absent headers are a real, cheap hardening gap with a genuine (if low-impact) token-in-URL exposure surface (history/logs/shoulder-surfing). Worth fixing, but lands at low for this hobby-app threat model rather than medium.

confirmed.title: OAuth callback redirect target derived from cors_origins[0], coupling CORS list ordering to auth redirect
confirmed.description: After Google login the backend redirects the user (with their freshly-minted JWT in the URL) to `settings.cors_origins[0]`. cors_origins is parsed from CORS_ORIGINS_STR (config.py:65-79), whose compose default is "http://localhost:5173,http://localhost:3000". If prod CORS_ORIGINS_STR is left at default or lists localhost first, a real user's post-login redirect (carrying their access token) goes to localhost — at best a broken login, at worst the token is appended to a URL the user's browser may not control. The redirect destination should be the dedicated PUBLIC_BASE_URL setting (which exists and is used for email links, magic_link.py:100), not whatever happens to be first in the CORS allowlist.
confirmed.evidence:
auth.py:191  frontend_url = settings.cors_origins[0] if settings.cors_origins else "http://localhost:5173"
config.py:62  public_base_url: str = "http://localhost:5173"  # the correct value to use here
confirmed.recommendation: Use settings.public_base_url (already the canonical public frontend URL) for the OAuth callback redirect instead of cors_origins[0], so the redirect target is independent of CORS list ordering and matches the value already used for email links.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The cited code is verified exactly as described. backend/app/api/auth.py:191 derives the post-Google-OAuth redirect target (carrying the freshly-minted JWT in the query string at :192) from `settings.cors_origins[0]`, falling back to localhost. config.py:65 sets the compose/default `cors_origins_str = "http://localhost:5173,http://localhost:3000"`, parsed first-element-first at config.py:69-79. A dedicated `public_base_url` setting exists (config.py:62) and is already used as the canonical public frontend base for outbound email links (magic_link.py:100 `get_settings().public_base_url.rstrip("/")`). docker-compose.yml:44 wires PUBLIC_BASE_URL and :30 wires CORS_ORIGINS_STR, both defaulting to localhost and both env-overridable. So the structural claim — the OAuth redirect couples to CORS list ordering instead of the dedicated public URL — is genuinely correct, and the recommendation (use public_base_url) is the right fix.

However, the severity framing is overstated. (1) This is operator-config coupling, not an attacker-controlled primitive: cors_origins[0] comes from the operator's own env, so there is no open-redirect / token-exfil-to-attacker path. A curious/competitive friend cannot influence the redirect target. (2) The token only ever lands on an origin the operator themselves placed in their CORS allowlist. (3) The realistic failure mode is self-inflicted broken login, and only if the operator (a) actually enables Google OAuth in prod and (b) orders their CORS list with a non-frontend origin first or leaves it at the localhost default. Google OAuth is the secondary path — magic-link is primary and already uses public_base_url correctly, Google OAuth returns 501 when unconfigured (auth.py:130-134), and the deployment plan does not indicate Google credentials are provisioned. For a single-frontend app the operator would naturally list the real frontend origin, which would be cors_origins[0], so it usually works by accident.

Net: a real latent config-drift bug and code smell worth fixing (two settings describe the same public-frontend concept and can diverge), but not adversary-exploitable and gated behind operator misconfiguration on a secondary auth path. Appropriate severity for this 30-friend hobby app is low rather than medium; the 'token appended to a URL the browser may not control' phrasing implies an exploitability that the code does not exhibit.

confirmed.title: Live trajectory point dated with local date.today() instead of UTC date, mismatching snapshot dates
confirmed.description: The trajectory endpoint appends a synthetic 'live now' point dated date.today(), which returns the server's LOCAL calendar date, whereas every stored snapshot's captured_date is computed from utc_now().date() (snapshots.py:50). The de-dup logic at leaderboard.py:177 compares points[-1].captured_date == live_point.captured_date to decide whether to overwrite today's snapshot vs append. Around the UTC midnight boundary (server in a non-UTC zone), date.today() and the UTC-derived snapshot date can disagree, producing either a duplicate same-day point or an overwrite of the wrong day on the chart. This is a cosmetic/correctness issue on a dashboard sparkline, not a scoring or lock issue, but it is a (mild) violation of the system-wide UTC-date convention used for snapshots.
confirmed.evidence: from datetime import date  ...  live_point = RankSnapshotPoint(..., captured_date=date.today())  # snapshots.py uses today = utc_now().date()
confirmed.recommendation: Use utc_now().date() here to match the snapshot write path, so the live point's date is computed on the same (UTC) calendar the stored snapshots use.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding's factual claims are all verified against the actual code:
- backend/app/api/leaderboard.py:4 — `from datetime import date` (confirmed).
- backend/app/api/leaderboard.py:173 — the synthetic "live now" trajectory point is constructed with `captured_date=date.today()`, which returns the server's LOCAL calendar date (confirmed).
- backend/app/services/snapshots.py:50 — the snapshot WRITE path uses `today = utc_now().date()`, i.e. the UTC calendar date (confirmed).
- backend/app/api/leaderboard.py:177 — de-dup logic compares `points[-1].captured_date == live_point.captured_date` to decide overwrite-vs-append (confirmed).

So the two dates are derived on different calendars (local vs UTC). If the server's local zone differs from UTC, near the UTC-midnight boundary `date.today()` and the stored snapshot's UTC date can disagree, yielding either a duplicate same-day point or an overwrite of the wrong day. This is also a genuine violation of the project's explicit system-wide invariant ("Every datetime is timezone-aware UTC; use utc_now()"), and CLAUDE.md/MEMORY both call out the UTC-date convention. The recommendation (use `utc_now().date()`) is correct. It is the ONLY `date.today()`/`datetime.now()` usage in the backend, so it's a clear outlier.

However the impact is correctly bounded to LOW and does not rise under this threat model:
1. The deployment runs in UTC. The backend image is python:3.11-slim with no TZ env var, no tzdata install, and no /etc/localtime mount in the Dockerfile or docker-compose.yml — so the container's local time IS UTC and `date.today() == utc_now().date()` in practice. The bug only manifests if someone sets a non-UTC TZ on the container.
2. Even when triggered, the consequence is purely cosmetic: a single mis-dated/duplicated "live now" dot on a dashboard rank-trajectory sparkline (the endpoints power DwFinalPosition / per-row sparkline / public profile per their docstrings). The live point's position and total_points come from the live leaderboard regardless of the date label.
3. It does NOT touch scoring, prediction locking, lock/kickoff timing, prediction/score data integrity, the blind pool, or any IDOR/cheating/snooping vector. No friend can use this to cheat, snoop, or corrupt data.

Verdict: REAL but minor — a legitimate code-quality / convention bug warranting a one-line fix, with no security or data-integrity consequence under the stated hobby-app threat model. Keeping the auditor's self-assigned severity of low.

confirmed.title: Redundant standings/rankings recomputation inside the per-user group-position bonus
confirmed.description: calculate_group_position_bonus (called once per user from the leaderboard loop at scoring.py:672) calls get_predicted_group_standings, get_actual_group_standings, AND get_qualifying_third_place_teams. get_qualifying_third_place_teams internally calls get_actual_group_standings_with_warnings AGAIN, so actual standings are computed twice per user, and _resolve_fifa_rankings (a DB query) runs three-plus times per user. Across 30 users on the leaderboard cold path this multiplies into dozens of full group-fixture scans and rankings queries that all produce identical tournament-global results.
confirmed.evidence: scoring.py:419 `predicted, _ = await get_predicted_group_standings(...)` / :420 `actual = await get_actual_group_standings(...)` / :424 `qualifying_thirds = await get_qualifying_third_place_teams(...)`; standings.py:546 inside get_qualifying_third_place_teams_with_warnings: `standings, group_warnings = await get_actual_group_standings_with_warnings(session)` (second computation of the same standings).
confirmed.recommendation: Compute actual group standings + qualifying thirds + FIFA rankings once per leaderboard build and thread them through to calculate_group_position_bonus, rather than recomputing per user. The user-specific part (predicted standings) is the only thing that legitimately varies per user.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The code clearly supports the claim. In backend/app/services/scoring.py:419-424, calculate_group_position_bonus makes three standings calls: get_predicted_group_standings (per-user, legit), get_actual_group_standings (tournament-global), and get_qualifying_third_place_teams. get_actual_group_standings is a thin wrapper over get_actual_group_standings_with_warnings (standings.py:360-365), and get_qualifying_third_place_teams -> get_qualifying_third_place_teams_with_warnings calls get_actual_group_standings_with_warnings AGAIN at standings.py:546 — so actual standings are computed twice per user. _resolve_fifa_rankings (standings.py:309-319, backed by a real DB query get_fifa_rankings, bonus.py:80-84, no memoization) runs at standings.py:424, :520, and :554, i.e. ~4 times per user, all producing identical global results except the per-user predicted standings. calculate_group_position_bonus is invoked once per user from scoring.py:672 inside the per-user leaderboard loop (leaderboard.py:138-139). The redundancy and the recommendation (thread shared actual standings + thirds + rankings through once per build) are accurate.

However, the 'high' rating is too aggressive for this app. Mitigating factors: (1) a 30s TTL cache (leaderboard.py:42, 117-125) means the cold rebuild runs at most once per 30s window, not per request; (2) ~30 active users; (3) the repeated work is a handful of indexed SELECTs over tiny tables (group fixtures ~104, teams ~48, fifa_rankings 211 rows). Each 'full group-fixture scan' is trivial. There is zero data-integrity, blind-pool, IDOR, or tampering consequence — results are byte-identical regardless of how many times the global standings are recomputed. Under the proportionate hobby-app/30-user threat model this is a genuine but minor efficiency cleanup, not a high-severity issue. I lowered it to low: real, easily fixed, negligible practical impact and no security/integrity dimension.

confirmed.title: Phase-1 receipt batch recomputes per user and runs serially inside the scheduler tick
confirmed.description: At the moment phase1_deadline passes, every scheduler tick (every 60s) calls send_phase1_receipts, which loops over all active users and for each builds the full receipt (multiple per-user queries) and awaits an external Resend HTTP send serially. Idempotency rows prevent re-sending, so after the first successful pass it no-ops — but the FIRST pass blocks that tick for the duration of ~30 sequential email round-trips (each up to a 20s httpx timeout, plus retry backoff on transient failures). Because the scheduler is a single asyncio task, a slow/failing Resend during that window also delays the in-tick score sync. Any user whose send keeps failing (permanent 4xx) is retried every single tick forever, re-building their receipt each time.
confirmed.evidence: score_scheduler.py:68 `await _maybe_send_phase1_receipts(session)` runs before the score-sync branch in the same tick; receipts.py:524 `for user in users:` ... :544 `result = await send_email(...)` (serial, per-user, with build_phase1_receipt per user at :535).
confirmed.recommendation: Run the receipt batch as its own background task rather than inline in the polling tick, or cap retries for permanent failures (record a 'failed' state so they aren't re-built every minute). For 30 users serial sends are acceptable once, but decouple it from the score-sync tick so a slow Resend can't stall live-score updates on match day.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding's factual claims are all accurate against the actual code, so it is not a false positive — but the impact is overstated for this threat model, so I lower it from medium to low.

Confirmed facts:
- score_scheduler.py:68 calls `_maybe_send_phase1_receipts` BEFORE the score-sync branch (lines 72-74) in the same `_run_one_tick`, which runs as a single asyncio task (run_scheduler_loop, line 132). So a slow receipt batch does delay in-tick score sync. (One nuance the finding omits: the receipt call is gated at receipts via `_maybe_send_phase1_receipts` lines 99-103 — only fires when an active competition has a past phase1_deadline, not literally every tick unconditionally.)
- receipts.py:524 `for user in users:` loops all active users; :535 `build_phase1_receipt` issues 3 per-user queries (`_load_group_predictions`, `_load_bracket_predictions`, `_load_bonus_predictions`, lines 95-135); :544 `await send_email(...)` is serial per user.
- email.py:107 uses `httpx.Timeout(20.0)` with retries (0s/1s/4s backoff, lines 149-154), so a worst-case hung Resend on 5xx/timeout could block ~20s+ per user.
- The "permanent 4xx retried every tick forever, rebuilding the receipt" claim is correct and even documented in the code's own comment (receipts.py:551-554): EmailSendError (which 4xx raises at email.py:130-134) does NOT write an idempotency row, so it `continue`s (line 560) and the next tick rebuilds and retries.
- Idempotency via the EmailSend pre-load query (lines 500-505) means after a successful first pass it no-ops — confirmed.

Why I lower to low rather than keep medium:
1. The blocking "first pass" is a single moment in tournament history (when phase1_deadline passes), not a recurring match-day load.
2. At that exact moment the receipt copy itself states "the first match kicks off in about an hour" (receipts.py:206) — i.e. the deadline structurally precedes the first kickoff by ~an hour, so `has_active_or_imminent_match` (score_sync.py:42, pre-kickoff buffer only 10 min) returns False and there is no live score to sync anyway. The finding's headline worry — "can't stall live-score updates on match day" — does not materialize, because no match is live during the receipt window.
3. Happy-path Resend latency for 30 users is ~10-15s total, a one-time delay with nothing live to sync.
4. Permanent-4xx retries fail fast (no 20s timeout, no backoff — email.py:130-134 raises immediately), so the steady-state cost of a bad address is ~3 queries + one quick HTTP call + log spam per tick for a handful of users among 30 — negligible.

It is a genuine architectural coupling and a real "rebuild-and-retry-forever" inefficiency worth the recommended decoupling/retry-cap, but under this hobby-app, 30-user, blind-pool/cheat-focused threat model it is operational hygiene (log noise + a harmless one-time delay), not a data-integrity, cheating, or snooping risk. Hence: real, but low.

Cited files: backend/app/services/score_scheduler.py:45-116; backend/app/services/receipts.py:476-583; backend/app/services/email.py:107-154; backend/app/services/score_sync.py:37-78.

confirmed.title: Missing composite indexes on the hottest filter pairs (predictions.user_id+phase, fixtures.status, fixtures.stage)
confirmed.description: The dominant query shape is `MatchPrediction WHERE user_id=? JOIN Fixture WHERE status=FINISHED` and `TeamPrediction WHERE user_id=? AND phase=?`. user_id is indexed but phase is not, and Fixture.status / Fixture.stage have no index (only home_team/away_team/kickoff/external_id do). At 30 users * ~136 predictions and ~104 fixtures the tables are tiny enough that Postgres will seq-scan them in microseconds, so this is genuinely low impact at launch scale — but the recurring leaderboard/standings/advancement queries filter on Fixture.status=FINISHED and Fixture.stage repeatedly, so a partial or composite index would help the (frequent, cached-miss) cold rebuild and is cheap insurance.
confirmed.evidence: scoring.py:604-607 `.where(MatchPrediction.user_id == user_id, Fixture.status == MatchStatus.FINISHED)`; standings.py:379 `.where(Fixture.stage == 'group', Fixture.status == MatchStatus.FINISHED)`; no index on Fixture.status or .stage in models/fixture.py or the initial migration.
confirmed.recommendation: Only if PERF-1/PERF-3 don't already remove the hot loops: add a composite index on match_predictions(user_id, fixture_id) (covers the join+filter) and a partial index on fixtures(status) WHERE status='finished' / fixtures(stage). Skip if you collapse the recompute to a few set-based queries, since the planner will scan the tiny tables fine.

I independently confirmed every factual claim in the finding by reading the cited code and all 13 migrations.

INDEX CLAIMS (accurate): The initial migration backend/alembic/versions/f06b6a2077d3_initial_schema.py creates indexes on fixtures.home_team/away_team/kickoff/external_id (lines 60-63), match_predictions.user_id/fixture_id (95-96), and team_predictions.user_id/team (127-128). There is NO index on fixtures.status, fixtures.stage, match_predictions.phase, or team_predictions.phase in ANY migration. The model declarations match: fixture.py:42 (stage) and :47 (status) have no index; prediction.py:35,72 (phase) have no index=True. So the claim "user_id indexed but phase is not, and Fixture.status/.stage have no index" is verbatim correct.

QUERY SHAPES (accurate): scoring.py:604-607 is exactly `.where(MatchPrediction.user_id == user_id, Fixture.status == MatchStatus.FINISHED)` over a join. standings.py:380-381 is exactly `.where(Fixture.stage == "group", Fixture.status == MatchStatus.FINISHED)`. I found 6+ additional Fixture.status==FINISHED / Fixture.stage filter sites across services, plus real phase filters at standings.py:481, receipts.py:124, bracket_exposure.py:302. These are genuine recurring filters.

HOT-PATH AMPLIFICATION (confirmed): leaderboard.py:138-139 loops over every active user and calls calculate_user_points, so on a cache miss the status-filtered join at scoring.py:600-607 runs ~30 times. The auditor's "cold rebuild" framing is correct.

WHY IT STILL STAYS LOW: The config (worldcup2026.yml: teams: 48) yields ~104 fixtures and 30 users * ~136 predictions. These tables are trivially small; Postgres seq-scans them in microseconds, and a composite/partial index would shave negligible time even on a cold rebuild. The finding itself acknowledges this ("genuinely low impact at launch scale... cheap insurance") and conditions the recommendation on PERF-1/PERF-3 not already collapsing the loops. This is also a pure performance/caching note: it does not touch the adversarial threat model at all — it cannot enable cheating, blind-pool snooping, IDOR, or data corruption. The finding is REAL in that it accurately describes the code and is a legitimate (if optional) optimization, but it is correctly self-rated low and is arguably the most marginal tier of useful. I leave severity at low rather than raising or refuting.

confirmed.title: Background scheduler creates a second SQLAlchemy engine / connection pool
confirmed.description: _make_session_factory builds its own create_async_engine separate from the app's module-level engine, so the process holds two independent asyncpg pools (default ~5+10 each). At 30 users on one worker this is harmless and even slightly isolates scheduler load from request load, but it doubles idle connection count against Postgres for no functional reason and the engine is never disposed. Worth noting for the deployment's max_connections budget on a small VPS.
confirmed.evidence: score_scheduler.py:41 `engine = create_async_engine(db_url)` (a fresh engine, distinct from database.py:44 `engine = get_async_engine()`); the request path uses async_session_maker bound to the app engine.
confirmed.recommendation: Reuse the app's async_session_maker (import from database.py) in the scheduler instead of constructing a second engine, or explicitly size both pools small (pool_size=2) so two pools don't exhaust Postgres max_connections under any future multi-worker config.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
All factual claims verified against the actual code.

1. Second engine: backend/app/services/score_scheduler.py:41 `engine = create_async_engine(db_url)` inside `_make_session_factory` builds a fresh engine, entirely separate from the app's module-level engine at backend/app/database.py:44 `engine = get_async_engine()`. The scheduler's session_factory is created at run_scheduler_loop start (score_scheduler.py:125) and never references database.py's `async_session_maker`. So the process genuinely holds two independent SQLAlchemy engines, each with its own asyncpg-backed QueuePool. CONFIRMED.

2. Default pool sizing: neither engine passes pool_size/max_overflow (grep found no such kwargs anywhere), so both use SQLAlchemy defaults (pool_size=5, max_overflow=10). Two pools. CONFIRMED.

3. Engine never disposed: scheduler_lifespan (score_scheduler.py:153-168) only sets stop_event and cancels the task in finally; it never calls engine.dispose(). CONFIRMED (though at process exit this is moot).

4. Deployment context: docker-compose.yml:63 and backend/Dockerfile:22 run a single uvicorn process with no --workers, and db is plain postgres:16-alpine with default max_connections=100. So one process holds both pools; with lazy pool fill the realistic idle connection count is a handful, far from exhausting 100. CONFIRMED single-worker.

Assessment: the finding is real and accurately described, and the author already rates it low and even notes the minor upside (scheduler load isolation). Under the stated threat model (30 friends, single worker, behind Cloudflare) it is not a security, cheating, snooping, IDOR, or data-integrity issue at all — it is a minor connection-budget/code-smell observation appropriate to the 'Backend Performance & Caching' dimension. The two pools cannot plausibly exhaust Postgres max_connections at this scale, and the recommendation (reuse async_session_maker or cap pool_size small) is proportionate and not enterprise-overkill. Keeping it at low: a legitimate, correctly-rated minor finding. Confidence reduced slightly from 1.0 only because the practical impact is near-zero, making 'low' arguably generous (info-level), but low is defensible.

confirmed.title: All ~260 flag-icons SVGs eagerly inlined into the JS bundle as raw strings
confirmed.description: flagSvgs.ts uses `import.meta.glob('/node_modules/flag-icons/flags/4x3/*.svg', { eager: true, query: '?raw', import: 'default' })`. `eager: true` means EVERY 4x3 flag SVG (flag-icons 7.5.0 ships ~260 country flags, many with detailed coats of arms) is inlined as a JavaScript string into the chunk that imports this module. PnFlag and PnAxisFlag both pull it, and those are transitively imported by the dashboard, predictions wizard, results, profile and bracket routes. So the first flag-using route a player visits downloads the full set even though a World Cup has 48 teams and any single screen shows at most ~50 flags. The flag-icons 4x3 raw set is on the order of 1.5-2 MB uncompressed (a few hundred KB gzipped) of pure data shipped to every player on a phone. The component's own comment acknowledges this trade-off ('all 271 SVGs land in the JS bundle as strings'). CLAUDE.md still describes PnFlag as lightweight '2/3-stripe gradient placeholders', so this regression isn't reflected in the docs.
confirmed.evidence: const flagSvgs = import.meta.glob<string>('/node_modules/flag-icons/flags/4x3/*.svg', { eager: true, query: '?raw', import: 'default' });
confirmed.recommendation: Drop `eager: true` so the glob becomes a map of lazy import() factories, and have rawFlagSvg/flagDataUrl resolve only the codes actually needed (await the factory, cache the result). Even simpler given the closed team set: at build time, narrow the glob to only the ISO codes that can appear (teamCodes.ts already maps team->code), or generate a small static module containing just those flags. Either approach cuts the flag payload by ~80% and removes hundreds of KB from the first flag-route load.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding is technically accurate and I could not refute it. Verified against actual code:

CODE: frontend/src/lib/utils/flagSvgs.ts:17-21 uses `import.meta.glob<string>('/node_modules/flag-icons/flags/4x3/*.svg', { eager: true, query: '?raw', import: 'default' })` exactly as cited. With Vite's eager glob, every matched file is compiled into a static top-level import and inlined into the importing chunk as a raw string — there is no per-key tree-shaking, so all SVGs ship even though rawFlagSvg/flagDataUrl only ever look up ~48-57 codes.

SIZE (empirically verified): I pulled flag-icons@7.5.0 via `npm pack` and extracted it. The 4x3 set is exactly 271 SVG files totaling 1,910 KB (1.91 MB) uncompressed and ~600 KB gzipped as a raw concatenation. This matches the finding's "~1.5-2 MB uncompressed, a few hundred KB gzipped." PnFlag.svelte:10-12 even self-documents this: "all 271 SVGs land in the JS bundle as strings."

ROUTE COVERAGE (verified): PnFlag is statically imported by src/routes/results/+page.svelte, results/[fixture_id], predictions/+page.svelte, profile/[userId], and by dashboard widgets (DwMatchTable, DwChampionPodium, PnBubbleGrid, PnKnockoutBracket, PnBracketMatch). The home route src/routes/+page.svelte statically imports DashGroupStage/DashboardKO/DashboardPost which transitively pull PnFlag. So the first flag-using route ships the full set, as claimed.

DOCS MISMATCH (verified): CLAUDE.md still describes PnFlag as "2/3-stripe gradient placeholders," but the component now inlines real flag-icons artwork. The finding's note about the doc regression is correct.

RECOMMENDATION FEASIBILITY (verified): teamCodes.ts contains a closed finite team set (~57 mappings for a 48-team World Cup), so the suggested build-time narrowing to only needed codes, or dropping eager:true for lazy import() factories, is realistic.

SEVERITY ADJUSTMENT (high -> low): The reported "high" is too strong for this threat model. The audit's priorities are cheating/tampering, snooping/IDOR, data integrity, and basic web hygiene — this is a pure front-end payload inefficiency with zero security or data-integrity impact and violates none of the five system invariants. The realistic cost is a one-time, browser-cached, Cloudflare/Brotli-compressed couple-hundred-KB on the first flag route for 30 friends; the PnFlag author explicitly judged the trade-off acceptable for this audience. It is a real, easily-fixable waste worth noting, but proportionate to a hobby app it is low severity, not high.

confirmed.title: Leaderboard 60s poll silently resets the user's Phase I/II tab and flashes a loading state
confirmed.description: The leaderboard route starts a 60s poll (startPolling(60000)). Every cycle pollLiveData() runs: it sets leaderboardLoading=true (line 98), then on success sets leaderboardPhase='overall' (line 108). The page's tab buttons bind class:on to $leaderboardPhase, and the rendered point columns are computed via matchPts/exactPts/etc. keyed on $leaderboardPhase. So if a player taps 'Phase I' to inspect group-stage standings, up to 60 seconds later the poll silently snaps them back to 'Overall' and recomputes every row's columns — their selection is lost mid-read. Separately, leaderboardLoading=true on every poll makes the header flip to 'LOADING…' once a minute even when data is already on screen, a visible flicker. The phase reset is the clearer defect: live polling and the phase filter are entangled, but the poll only ever returns overall data so it clobbers the filter rather than refreshing it in place.
confirmed.recommendation: Don't mutate leaderboardPhase from the poll. Either (a) poll the currently-selected phase, or (b) keep the poll writing to overall but store live data in a separate store and let the displayed table stay on whatever phase the user picked. Also gate the loading flag: only set leaderboardLoading on the FIRST poll (when leaderboard is empty), not on every refresh, so the header doesn't flash 'LOADING…' every 60s over already-rendered rows.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL — every claimed mechanic is present in the code, and the impact is actually slightly broader than the auditor stated.

Store (frontend/src/lib/stores/leaderboard.ts):
- pollLiveData() sets leaderboardLoading.set(true) at line 98, then on success does leaderboardPhase.set('overall') at line 108 with the comment "// Reset to overall since polling returns overall data".
- startPolling(60000) (lines 81-86) immediately calls pollLiveData() and then setInterval(pollLiveData, 60000). There is NO gating to live matches — polling runs unconditionally the whole time the page is mounted.

Route (frontend/src/routes/leaderboard/+page.svelte):
- onMount calls startPolling(60000) when authenticated (line 29); onDestroy calls stopPolling() (line 34).
- Tab buttons bind class:on={$leaderboardPhase === 'overall'|'phase_1'|'phase_2'} (lines 149-151, dup at 277-279), and handlePhaseChange -> setPhase -> fetchLeaderboard sets leaderboardPhase.
- The per-row columns are computed reactively from the store: exactPts/outcomePts/bonusPts/bracketPts(r.breakdown, $leaderboardPhase) at lines 214-217; the helpers (lines 57-81) branch on phase. Because $leaderboardPhase is an auto-subscribed store, the whole {#each} re-renders when the poll resets it.
- The header flips on the loading flag: line 182 "{#if $leaderboardLoading}LOADING…{:else if $lastCalculated}Updated …{/if}", so leaderboardLoading=true every 60s makes the header flash LOADING over already-rendered rows.

Backend confirms the poll is overall-only and the breakdown is full: scores.py:44-50 /scores/poll calls calculate_leaderboard(session) with NO phase arg. In services/leaderboard.py the entry's total_points = phase-filtered points (line 150) and entries are SORTED and POSITIONED by those phase points (lines 159-172), while breakdown is "Full breakdown (always includes all phases)" (line 151). Therefore when a user is on Phase I and the poll fires, the table doesn't just recompute columns — it re-sorts rows, re-numbers positions, and changes the Total column to overall values, and snaps the tab highlight back to Overall. The user's whole selected view is clobbered, up to once a minute, mid-read.

So the finding is accurate and the symptom is real, reproducible, and on a primary page. It is NOT a security/cheating/integrity issue (no data corruption, no blind-pool leak, no IDOR, no lock bypass) — it is a frontend interaction/responsiveness defect, which matches the reporting dimension. Under this hobby-app threat model I'd rate it low rather than medium: it's an annoyance with zero data harm, and the fix is trivial (don't mutate leaderboardPhase from the poll; write live data to a separate store or re-poll the selected phase; and only set leaderboardLoading on the first/empty load). Real bug, low severity.

confirmed.title: Every route/dashboard refetches all data on each navigation; module-store caching is never used as a cache
confirmed.description: Stores are module-level singletons, so data survives navigation — but no fetch function honors that. fetchAllFixtures/fetchLeaderboard/fetchMatchPredictions unconditionally hit the network and call .set() on each onMount. Navigating dashboard -> leaderboard -> dashboard re-runs DashGroupStage.onMount and refetches fixtures + leaderboard + predictions + rank trajectory from scratch; the same is true for DashboardPre/Between/KO/Post, the predictions wizard (onMount Promise.all of three fetches), and results. With data-sveltekit-preload-data='hover' in app.html, simply hovering nav links can also kick module loads early. At 30 users this is harmless backend load, but it means the dashboard shows a fresh loading pass on every return visit instead of rendering the cached data instantly and revalidating. The phase store already half-solves this elsewhere via a hasLoadedPhase guard in +layout.svelte:34.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a lightweight freshness guard to the fetch functions (e.g. a module-level lastFetched timestamp; skip the network call if the store is non-empty and fetched < N seconds ago, or expose a forceRefresh flag). Render the cached store value immediately on remount and revalidate in the background. This removes the per-navigation loading flash without changing the data model.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Every factual claim in the finding is verified against the actual code; I could not refute any of them.

- frontend/src/lib/components/panini/dashboard/DashGroupStage.svelte:38-47 — onMount unconditionally calls fetchAllFixtures(), fetchLeaderboard(), fetchMatchPredictions() and awaits getMyRankTrajectory(7). Snippet matches exactly.
- The three store fetch functions have NO freshness guard: fetchAllFixtures (fixtures.ts:44-56), fetchLeaderboard (leaderboard.ts:45-65), fetchMatchPredictions (predictions.ts:77-90) each set a loading flag, hit the API, and call .set() every time. There is no lastFetched timestamp, no "skip if non-empty", no forceRefresh flag anywhere in these stores. The stores are module-level writables (singletons), so data does survive navigation but is always overwritten on remount.
- "Same is true for DashboardPre/Between/KO/Post": confirmed — DashboardPre.svelte:50-53, DashboardBetween.svelte:42-49, DashboardKO.svelte:48-54, DashboardPost.svelte:53-60 all refetch in onMount with no guard.
- Predictions wizard: routes/predictions/+page.svelte:190-204 — onMount does Promise.all([fetchMatchPredictions, fetchGroupFixtures, fetchBracketPredictions]) (plus three more if Phase 2 active), unconditionally.
- Results page: routes/results/+page.svelte:38-45 — onMount Promise.all refetches fetchAllFixtures + fetchMatchPredictions.
- data-sveltekit-preload-data="hover": confirmed at app.html:16.
- The phase store half-solving it via a guard: confirmed at +layout.svelte:34-37, where a hasLoadedPhase boolean prevents refetching phase status. routes/+layout.ts only sets ssr=false and returns {}, so there is no route-level load() caching — the description is accurate.

So the code unambiguously does what the finding claims, and the recommended fix (module-level freshness timestamp / render-cached-then-revalidate) is appropriate.

On severity/relevance: this is a pure performance/UX observation and carries ZERO security weight under the stated threat model. It does not enable cheating, snooping (blind pool / IDOR), or admin escalation, and it does not threaten data integrity — if anything, always overwriting the singleton stores with fresh server data is mildly SAFER than a stale cache. The finding itself acknowledges "At 30 users this is harmless backend load." The real-world impact is a loading flash on return navigation. For a 30-user hobby app behind Cloudflare this is cosmetic. It is correctly rated low (and is arguably info-level from a security standpoint), but since it accurately describes real code and is a legitimate finding for the auditor's assigned dimension (Frontend Performance & Responsiveness), I am marking it real at low rather than a false positive. It should not influence any security decision.

Optionally, on the mobile track only render the active page +/- 1 round (key off `page`) instead of all rounds, since they're translated off-screen anyway. Low priority — the bracket size is fixed and small.

Confirmed against the actual code:

confirmed.title: Dependency versions pinned with lower bounds only (>=), no lockfile for backend

The scary example it cites (python-jose algorithm-confusion) is already mitigated in this codebase. app/dependencies.py:69 decodes JWTs with an explicit allow-list `algorithms=[settings.jwt_algorithm]` (HS256, config.py:29).
