confirmed.recommendation: Confirm the production deployment uses a non-reload command (the Dockerfile CMD at backend/Dockerfile:22 already omits --reload, which is good). If this single compose file is reused in prod, parameterize the command so --reload is dev-only.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (factually), correctly rated info under the threat model.

Cited code is accurate. docker-compose.yml:63 is exactly `command: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload`, and the backend service bind-mounts source (docker-compose.yml:46-51: `./backend/app:/app/app`, scripts, tests, alembic, config). backend/Dockerfile:22 CMD omits --reload (`CMD ["uvicorn", "app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]`), so the compose `command:` overrides the image default with the dev flag.

I tried to refute by hunting for a separate prod compose / override that would exempt production — and the evidence cut the OTHER way, strengthening the finding rather than refuting it:
- There is exactly ONE compose file (git ls-files shows only docker-compose.yml; no docker-compose.override.yml, no docker-compose.prod.yml, no .env.prod).
- The nginx and cloudflared services carry `profiles: [prod]` (lines 112-113, 125-126) but the `backend` service has NO profile, so it starts under every profile including prod.
- Makefile:149 `deploy: ... docker compose --profile prod up -d --build` and Makefile:177 `vps-up: docker --context predictor compose --profile prod up -d` are the actual production bring-up commands. `--profile prod` only ADDS nginx+cloudflared; it still launches the unprofiled `backend` with the line-63 `--reload` command and the source bind-mounts.
- The deployment-plan memory states the team "made docker-compose.yml prod-ready" (parameterized secrets, loopback ports, restart policies, cloudflared, CF-Connecting-IP) — i.e., the SAME single file is the prod artifact, with no command de-dev-ification done.

So the auditor's hedge ("likely the dev compose", "presumably a separate compose/run") is incorrect: production really does run the backend with --reload and live working-tree bind-mounts.

Why it still matters only at info level for THIS threat model: an authenticated, somewhat-technical friend behind Cloudflare Tunnel cannot write to the VPS filesystem (`./backend/app`), so --reload + bind-mounts give them no path to cheat (edit predictions post-lock), snoop the blind pool, perform IDOR, corrupt data, or shift lock timing. The impact is purely operational/cleanliness: filesystem-watch overhead on a 4GB box, restart-on-write semantics during traffic, and the running code being the bind-mounted working tree rather than the image-baked copy (a stray uncommitted edit or a git pull on the VPS alters live behavior). The recommendation (confirm prod is non-reload, or parameterize the command so --reload is dev-only — e.g. ${UVICORN_RELOAD:-}) is sound and cheap. Severity info is correct; I considered raising to low because it genuinely IS in prod rather than merely suspected, but with zero security impact under the stated adversary model, info is the right call.

confirmed.title: DashboardBetween phase-2 bracket progress is hardcoded to 0 (TODO), so the 'Re-pick bracket' funnel always reads 0% updated
confirmed.description: On the between-phases dashboard the funnel hero's progress and the 'Bracket has unsaved changes' alert are both driven by `bracketFilled = 0; // TODO: wire to bracket store`. So during the entire Phase 1->2 window the hero shows '0 / 32 updated' even after a user has re-picked and saved their Phase II bracket in the wizard, and the unsaved-bracket alert (gated on `bracketFilled > 0`) can never fire. The wizard itself works; only this dashboard's reflection of it is stubbed, which makes the between-phases screen feel like it isn't tracking the user's progress. Not a correctness/integrity issue, but it's the one phase whose dashboard understates the user's real state.
confirmed.evidence: DashboardBetween.svelte:171 `$: bracketFilled = 0; // TODO: wire to bracket store`; 183 `{#if bracketFilled > 0 && bracketFilled < TOTAL_P2_BRACKET_SLOTS}`; 199 `progressValue={bracketFilled}`.
confirmed.recommendation: Wire bracketFilled to the Phase 2 bracket store (countBracketSlotsFilled on $phase2BracketPrediction, the same helper the wizard uses) so the between-phases funnel and alert reflect real progress, mirroring how DashboardPre composes its progress.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL, severity correctly info. The cited code matches exactly. DashboardBetween.svelte:171 hardcodes `$: bracketFilled = 0; // TODO: wire to bracket store`. Because of that constant: (a) the funnel hero at line 199 `progressValue={bracketFilled}` with `progressTotal={TOTAL_P2_BRACKET_SLOTS}` (=32, line 58/200) permanently renders "0 / 32 updated" during the entire between-phases window, even after the user re-picks and saves their Phase 2 bracket; and (b) the "Bracket has unsaved changes" alert at line 183 is gated on `bracketFilled > 0 && bracketFilled < TOTAL_P2_BRACKET_SLOTS`, which can never be true when bracketFilled is permanently 0, so the alert never fires.

The recommendation is sound and the building blocks exist: `phase2BracketPrediction` store (predictions.ts:41, set at :202) and the `countBracketSlotsFilled` helper (bracketProgress.ts:32). The wizard (routes/predictions/+page.svelte:302, 496) and DashboardPre (DashboardPre.svelte:87) both already use that helper, so the "mirror DashboardPre" comparison is accurate. One minor nuance the auditor didn't flag: the shared helper counts all 63 KO slots (BRACKET_TOTAL_SLOTS = 63) whereas the Between hero denominator is 32, so a real wiring would need to reconcile the denominator — but this doesn't undermine the finding.

DashboardBetween is genuinely reachable: +page.svelte:36 dispatches it for the `between_phases` ux phase. So the stub is live, not dead code.

Threat-model impact: this is a pure frontend display stub. It does not touch the prediction write path, server-side lock enforcement, the blind pool, Phase 1/2 data separation, or any server-scoped data — it reads no other user's data and cannot corrupt anything. It cannot enable a friend to cheat, snoop, or break data integrity. The only effect is that the between-phases dashboard understates the user's own bracket progress and one helper alert never appears. This is a cosmetic/UX accuracy gap, correctly classified as info. The original auditor's own framing ("Not a correctness/integrity issue") is consistent with the code.

refuted.title: bcrypt 72-byte truncation not handled; password max_length allows silent truncation
refuted.whyRefuted:
The cited code is accurate. backend/app/dependencies.py:33-38 — get_password_hash calls bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode("utf-8"), bcrypt.gensalt()) with no pre-hash and no length pre-check. backend/app/schemas/auth.py:17 (UserCreate.password) and :57 (PasswordChange.new_password) both use Field(min_length=8, max_length=100). The dependency pinned in backend/pyproject.toml:14 is "bcrypt>=4.0.0" — the standalone pyca/bcrypt library used directly (no passlib). Callers are register() (auth.py:64) and change_password() (auth.py:220), neither wrapping the hash call in error handling.

So the silent-truncation mechanism is real: a user choosing a >72-byte passphrase would have the tail ignored by bcrypt. But this does NOT genuinely matter under the stated threat model (~30 tech-savvy friends, self-hosted behind Cloudflare):

1. No impact on the things that MATTER. Truncation cannot help a friend cheat (edit predictions/scores/paid/admin), snoop on others' predictions, IDOR, or corrupt data. The only consequence is that a single user's own >72-byte password loses entropy beyond byte 72 — relevant only to offline hash-cracking, which is out of scope here (the password_hash column isn't exposed via the API; the adversary is a curious friend hitting endpoints, not someone with a DB dump). The "two passwords sharing the first 72 bytes verify equal" case requires a user to deliberately set a 72+ byte password and an attacker to guess the first 72 bytes — not a realistic scenario for this audience.

2. The finding's concrete operational claim is factually wrong for the library in use. pyca/bcrypt 4.x does NOT raise on passwords longer than 72 bytes — it silently truncates (which is the finding's own first premise). It raises ValueError only on embedded NUL bytes, not on length. So the predicted "500 on registration with a long password" will not occur, and the finding internally contradicts itself (can't both silently truncate and 500 on the same long input). (A genuinely separate latent crash exists — a password containing a \x00 byte would raise an unhandled 500 — but that is a different bug, a trivial self-DoS at registration with no security/cross-user impact, and not what this finding describes.)

The reporter themselves labeled this "Minor for this audience" and a "latent correctness/security wart," and rated it low. That self-assessment is honest. Net: the code supports the truncation claim but it is immaterial under this threat model, and the load-bearing severity rationale (the 500 risk) is incorrect for pyca/bcrypt 4.x. I refute it as a practical/false-positive finding and lower severity to info. The recommendation (cap at 72 bytes or pre-hash with SHA-256, plus a regression test) is reasonable defensive hygiene but not a real security issue for this app.

refuted.title: OAuth allow_insecure_http and API docs are tied to DEBUG, which defaults safely but is operator-overridable
refuted.whyRefuted:
All cited code is accurate: backend/app/api/auth.py:45 `allow_insecure_http=settings.debug`; backend/app/main.py:36-37 gate `/api/docs` and `/api/redoc` on `settings.debug`; backend/app/config.py:20 `debug: bool = False` (safe default); backend/.env.example line 1 `DEBUG=true`. So the literal observations hold.

However, the claimed harm scenario ("if the operator copies .env.example to prod, OAuth accepts insecure HTTP and the OpenAPI docs go public") does not survive the actual deployment path:

1. docker-compose.yml:29 sets `DEBUG=${DEBUG:-false}` and there is NO `env_file:` directive in the compose backend service — backend/.env.example is never loaded into the container. The deployed DEBUG value comes from host shell / a top-level `.env` substitution and defaults to false, completely independent of backend/.env.example. The documented prod path (Hetzner + docker-compose + Cloudflare Tunnel, per project memory) is this compose path.

2. config.py loads `env_file=".env"`, but the shipped file is named `.env.example`. Even bare-metal, it only takes effect after a deliberate `cp .env.example .env`, which no doc instructs for prod; .gitignore (lines 51-55) ignores all `.env*`, and the Dockerfile `COPY . .` does not produce a `.env`.

3. Net result: two independent safe defaults in the real deploy path (compose `:-false` plus config `= False`). The original auditor did not account for the compose `${DEBUG:-false}` layer, which makes residual risk even smaller than stated.

Under the threat model (curious tech-savvy friends, ~30 users, behind Cloudflare TLS), the OAuth `allow_insecure_http` toggle is moot (TLS terminates at Cloudflare) and exposing OpenAPI docs would at most reveal an endpoint map already discoverable from the SvelteKit client — it grants no authorization bypass and lets nobody cheat, snoop, or corrupt data. I mark isReal=false: this is documentation hygiene, not a security defect; the code default is correct and the deployment mechanism reinforces it. I keep adjustedSeverity=info (matching the original rating) and falsePositive=false because flipping .env.example to DEBUG=false is a reasonable, harmless suggestion — it is just not an operationally meaningful finding. Confidence 0.8 rather than higher because I cannot inspect the operator's actual VPS host .env.

refuted.title: Per-user point breakdown is exposed for arbitrary user_id with only optional auth
refuted.whyRefuted:
The cited code is accurate: backend/app/api/leaderboard.py:130-135 defines get_user_breakdown with OptionalUser and returns the full PointBreakdown for any user_id, and there is no global auth middleware (backend/app/main.py:27-57 adds only CORS), so it is indeed anonymous-reachable. However, the finding's substance is refuted by surrounding code:

1) The same granular breakdown is ALREADY public for the whole field. GET /leaderboard/ is also OptionalUser (leaderboard.py:94-100) and its LeaderboardResponse embeds the full PointBreakdown for every user via LeaderboardEntry.breakdown (schemas/leaderboard.py:172), populated at services/leaderboard.py:151 with the identical calculate_user_points() result (comment: "Full breakdown (always includes all phases)"). An anonymous caller already receives the complete per-user breakdown (exact scores, outcomes, hybrid bonus, per-phase splits) for all ~30 users in one /leaderboard/ request. The /breakdown/{user_id} endpoint exposes nothing additional.

2) The claimed inconsistency with peer endpoints is wrong. The closest per-user endpoints — GET /users/{user_id}/profile (users.py:157-161) and GET /users/{user_id}/predictions (users.py:180-184) — also use OptionalUser and are anonymous-reachable. The auditor cited only /snapshots/{user_id} (which uses CurrentUser) as the baseline while ignoring the OptionalUser peers and the public leaderboard. The read-surface auth posture is genuinely mixed, not a clean "everything else requires auth."

3) No blind-pool or secret data leaks: the breakdown is aggregate scoring totals over already-scored matches; it cannot reveal an unlocked prediction, so it enables no cheating or snooping under the threat model.

Net: requiring CurrentUser here would close no real gap, because the identical data is one anonymous /leaderboard/ call away. This is a non-issue (info), not a low-severity finding — marking false positive.

refuted.title: Server-side goal cap (20) is looser than the UI cap (15)
refuted.whyRefuted:
VERDICT: Factually accurate, but NOT a real security finding (correctly self-described by the reporter as "not a vulnerability"). Severity confirmed at info.

What I verified:
1. Cited code matches exactly. backend/app/schemas/prediction.py:16-17 (MatchPredictionCreate) and :23-24 (MatchPredictionUpdate) both use `home_score: int = Field(ge=0, le=20)` / `away_score: int = Field(ge=0, le=20)`. Confirmed by direct Read.
2. The UI cap of 15 is real and live-enforced: frontend/src/routes/predictions/+page.svelte:377 `const MAX_GOALS = 15;`, applied on every keystroke via `clampScoreInput` (line 379) wired to `on:input` on the score inputs (lines 1162-1163, 1178-1179, 1373-1374, 1389-1390). So the 15-vs-20 mismatch the reporter describes genuinely exists.

Why it does not matter under the threat model:
- No cheating vector. The DB model (backend/app/models/prediction.py:33-34) only enforces `ge=0`; the schema's le=20 is actually stricter than the model. Submitting 16-20 via curl produces a syntactically valid but absurd prediction. The scoring path compares predicted vs actual scores purely by equality and outcome sign — backend services/scoring.py and the mirrored frontend matchBreakdown.ts:150-155 (`exact = home===actual && away===actual`; `sameOutcome` via outcomeSign). An impossible 18-goal prediction can at most score a correct OUTCOME (e.g. 18-0 still means a home win) and will never score the exact-score bonus against any realistic result. It cannot inflate the cheater's points beyond what an in-range prediction with the same outcome would earn, and it cannot affect any other user's score (rarity bonus is computed from outcome agreement, not from goal magnitude). So no integrity loss, no point distortion.
- No snoop / IDOR / lock-bypass / data-corruption dimension is touched by this field bound at all.
- No render breakage: scores are plain integers rendered as numbers (e.g. PnBubbleGrid clamps display cells with Math.min(4,...) for the heatmap; other cards just print the number). "Renders oddly" is the worst case and is purely cosmetic.

The reporter themselves rated it info and wrote "This is not a vulnerability." I agree on every point. The recommendation (align le=15 or document 20 as the server ceiling) is a reasonable consistency nit but is not a security issue for a 30-person hobby app. Marking isReal=false / falsePositive=true because it is not a genuine security finding, while affirming the factual claim is correct and the info severity is appropriate.

refuted.title: DATABASE_PASSWORD defaults to the literal "predictor" in both db and backend services
refuted.whyRefuted:
The factual claims are all accurate. I confirmed docker-compose.yml:8 reads `POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DATABASE_PASSWORD:-predictor}` and docker-compose.yml:27 reads `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://predictor:${DATABASE_PASSWORD:-predictor}@db:5432/predictor`. Both fall back to the literal `predictor`. I also confirmed the mitigating context the finding itself cites: Postgres is bound loopback-only (line 13 `"127.0.0.1:5432:5432"`), so it is not network-reachable. The backend .env.example (line 6) likewise ships `predictor:predictor` as the dev credential, so the weak default is a dev convenience, not a prod secret.

However, the finding does NOT genuinely matter under the stated threat model, so I mark it not-real (info-level hygiene, not an exploitable issue):

1. Wrong adversary. The threat model adversary is an authenticated, somewhat-technical FRIEND interacting through the web app over Cloudflare Tunnel. Exploiting a weak DB password requires host/loopback access to the VPS. Friends have no shell on the box (deployment plan: ufw default-deny inbound, only port 22, SSH key-only, root login off, no public ports — access is via Cloudflare Tunnel to nginx). There is no in-scope path from "curious friend" to the loopback Postgres port.

2. The "compromised sidecar / other service" scenario the finding raises does not exist here: this is a single-box deployment with backend/db/nginx/cloudflared, no untrusted co-tenant. If an attacker already has loopback access, they have reached the backend's own environment, which contains DATABASE_URL with the real credential regardless of its strength — password strength adds nothing at that point.

3. The "forgotten prod .env" failure mode is already mitigated by an existing control: the production deployment plan records that the prod `.env` was populated (DB password among the secrets) and copied to a password manager, and that docker-compose was made prod-ready with "parameterized secrets." So in the actual prod environment DATABASE_PASSWORD is set, not defaulting.

The recommendation (drop the `:-predictor` default so compose fails loudly on a missing var, and document DATABASE_PASSWORD in a prod-env checklist) is reasonable defense-in-depth hardening, and there is a minor documentation gap (DATABASE_PASSWORD is not listed in backend/.env.example). But it is hygiene, not a vulnerability a friend can use to cheat, snoop, or corrupt data. Per the rules of engagement (default to refuted when an existing control mitigates and the concern does not apply to this hobby app's actual adversary), this does not rise to a real security finding. Adjusted severity: info.

refuted.title: /leaderboard/breakdown/{user_id} exposes any user's full point breakdown to any caller
refuted.whyRefuted:
The cited code exists and behaves mechanically as described. backend/app/api/leaderboard.py:130-135 defines GET /breakdown/{user_id} with `_user: OptionalUser`; backend/app/dependencies.py:88-99,116 confirm OptionalUser returns None when no token is present, so the endpoint is callable unauthenticated, and backend/app/api/__init__.py:14 mounts the leaderboard router with no router-level auth dependency. So "unauthenticated, takes arbitrary user_id" is literally true.

However, the finding does not MATTER under the stated threat model, for three concrete reasons grounded in the code:

1) The identical data is ALREADY public via the main leaderboard. GET /leaderboard/ (leaderboard.py:94-117) is also OptionalUser/unauthenticated and returns LeaderboardResponse whose every entry embeds the full PointBreakdown (schemas/leaderboard.py:172 `breakdown: PointBreakdown`), populated for every user at services/leaderboard.py:151 with the comment "Full breakdown (always includes all phases)". The per-stage advancement buckets the finding worries about (round_of_32_points ... winner_points, schemas/leaderboard.py:120-154) are therefore already served in bulk to the same audience. /breakdown/{user_id} exposes a strict subset of what /leaderboard/ already exposes.

2) It is purely post-scoring aggregate of FINISHED matches. calculate_user_points (scoring.py:575-689) filters MatchPrediction joins on Fixture.status == MatchStatus.FINISHED (line 606) and only counts advancement points that are non-zero against already-resolved actual advancement (lines 654-658). It reveals zero un-locked picks, so it does not violate the blind-pool invariant, and being read-only it enables no tampering/cheating. The finding itself concedes this ("much weaker leak ... doesn't directly reveal un-locked picks").

3) The "hint at a rival's still-live bracket exposure" claim is weak: per-stage points reflect points already earned on RESOLVED stages, not the rival's pending picks for un-resolved rounds — which is the only thing that would confer a snooping advantage.

The one legitimate residual is that the leaderboard read surface as a whole is unauthenticated; but that is a property of the entire surface (main leaderboard, scoring-rules), not a per-endpoint IDOR specific to /breakdown/{user_id}, and behind Cloudflare for ~30 friends with the same data already public, the marginal exposure here is informational. I am not marking it a pure false positive (the unauthenticated read surface is a real, if minor, observation), but the finding as framed — a medium-severity "unauthenticated IDOR over per-user data" — overstates impact because an existing, broader public endpoint already serves the same data. Lowered to info.

refuted.title: Score-input cap is 20 in the API schema but documented/enforced as 15 in the UI
refuted.whyRefuted:
The code claim is accurate on both halves, but the finding does not genuinely matter under the stated threat model, so I downgrade it from a low security finding to informational.

Verified facts:
- Server schema caps at 20: backend/app/schemas/prediction.py:16-17 (MatchPredictionCreate) and :23-24 (MatchPredictionUpdate) both declare home_score/away_score as int = Field(ge=0, le=20). Confirmed by reading the file directly.
- Client caps at 15: frontend/src/routes/predictions/+page.svelte:377 declares `const MAX_GOALS = 15;`, enforced live via handleScoreInput (line 390: Math.max(0, Math.min(MAX_GOALS, ...))) and clampScoreInput on every keystroke (lines 379-387), with a comment (line 376) stating 'typing "16" instantly becomes "15"'. This matches CLAUDE.md's UI guideline. So the 20-vs-15 drift between server and client is real.

Why it does not matter under the threat model (cheating/snooping/integrity by a technical friend, ~30 users, behind Cloudflare):
- No cheating advantage. I read the scoring strategies (backend/app/services/scoring.py:124-240). FixedScoring and LogarithmicScoring award points solely from (a) correct outcome, (b) exact_score equality (home==final_home AND away==final_away), and (c) a rarity bonus driven only by correct_predictors/total_predictors counts. The magnitude of the goal count has zero effect on points beyond exact-match equality. A 16-20 prediction will essentially never equal a real football scoreline, and even hypothetically it would score deterministically with no inflation. The rarity bonus is gated by predictor agreement (f>=0.5 earns nothing), not by score size, so high scores cannot be used to game the bonus.
- No integrity or timing impact. A 16-20 value is still bounded, non-negative (ge=0), integer, and persisted/scored deterministically — no corruption, no data loss, no lock/kickoff shift.
- No snooping/blind-pool relevance. Score range is unrelated to visibility.
- The 'documented invariant' is a UI input-affordance note in CLAUDE.md ('capped at 15 ... enforced live in the input event'), not one of the five hard system invariants. The finding itself concedes 'This is not a security or data-integrity hole.'

Net: the only real consequence is cosmetic contract drift — a technical friend could POST a 16-20 score the UI wouldn't let others produce. The recommendation (set le=15 server-side) is reasonable code hygiene, but this is informational, not a security/integrity issue, and does not warrant 'low' severity under this threat model.

refuted.title: get_match_predictions awaits get_fixture_lock_view per row (per-request N+1 on the user's own predictions list)
refuted.whyRefuted:
The cited code exists exactly as described (predictions.py:165-189): one query fetches all rows, phase1_locked is computed once at line 165, then the loop at line 168 calls `await get_fixture_lock_view(session, fixture, phase1_locked=phase1_locked)` per row at line 169. However, the finding does NOT genuinely matter, and even refutes its own title.

Verified behavior of get_fixture_lock_view (locking.py:86-120):
- For group fixtures (the ~104 group predictions): phase1_locked is supplied (not None), so line 113-114's branch skips is_phase1_locked entirely. ZERO DB queries.
- For non-group/Phase 2 fixtures (the ~32 KO predictions): it only calls check_fixture_locked (locking.py:118, pure Python — a timedelta subtraction and utc_now() comparison) and get_time_until_lock (locking.py:120, also pure Python). ZERO DB queries.
- get_lock_minutes() (config.py:106-116) reads from get_tournament_config() which is @lru_cache-backed (config.py:100) — in-memory, no I/O.

So the loop performs ZERO additional database queries. The finding's TITLE claims "per-request N+1" but its own DESCRIPTION admits "this is mostly CPU, not extra queries" and "get_fixture_lock_view with phase1_locked supplied does no DB I/O" — the title and body contradict, and the body is correct. There is no N+1.

What actually remains is ~130 awaited coroutine calls per request, each doing a couple of arithmetic ops and a datetime comparison. Awaiting a coroutine that never hits I/O is effectively a function call with trivial event-loop bookkeeping (low-microsecond each). At the stated scale (10-20 concurrent users, ~30 total, self-hosted behind Cloudflare) the aggregate overhead is sub-millisecond and is dwarfed by the single real DB round-trip and response serialization. No measurable performance impact, and obviously no security/integrity/snooping concern (the threat model's actual stakes).

The recommendation to inline the pure check is a micro-optimization that would also duplicate the deliberately-centralized dual-lock logic (locking.py:93-104) governing data-integrity-critical lock timing — a maintainability regression for no real benefit. This is borderline by the auditor's own wording and amounts to a code-style preference, not a defect. Not a real finding at medium severity; at most an info-level note.

refuted.title: get_user_predictions calls get_fixture_lock_view per prediction row (blind-pool gate in a Python loop)
refuted.whyRefuted:
The cited code exists exactly as described. backend/app/api/users.py:210 `for pred, fixture in rows:` and :213 `locked, _ = await get_fixture_lock_view(session, fixture, phase1_locked=phase1_locked)` is a per-row awaited service call inside the blind-pool gate, and phase1_locked is computed once at users.py:207 via is_phase1_locked.

However, the finding does NOT genuinely matter and overstates severity at "medium":

1. No N+1 SQL. I read services/locking.py:86-120. get_fixture_lock_view's only DB access is `await is_phase1_locked(session)` at line 114, which is gated by `if phase1_locked is None`. Since the caller always passes phase1_locked (a bool, not None), that branch never runs. For non-group fixtures the `if fixture.stage == "group"` block is skipped entirely. The remaining work — check_fixture_locked (locking.py:27) and get_time_until_lock (locking.py:45) — is pure synchronous Python. get_lock_minutes() reads from get_tournament_config(), which is @lru_cache'd (config.py:100-101), so no YAML re-read. Net per-row cost is just coroutine creation/await overhead plus a couple of pure-function calls and a dict lookup: microseconds, zero I/O. The finding correctly concedes this ("the per-row cost is mostly coroutine overhead rather than SQL").

2. Bounded and tiny. This is World Cup 2026 (~104 fixtures total), so the loop is capped at ~104 iterations of sub-millisecond pure-Python work. At 30 users viewing a few profiles each, this is negligible. The finding itself says "At 30 users... this is fine; flagged for the pattern."

3. No security/integrity impact. This is a pure performance-style note, not a cheat/snoop/integrity vector. The blind-pool gate it sits inside is working correctly and server-side: lines 216-217 skip any fixture that is neither locked nor finished, so the visibility rule holds regardless of this performance detail.

The recommendation (hoist into a pure helper) is reasonable code cleanliness, but for a hobby app behind Cloudflare for 30 people, a no-SQL, sub-millisecond pure-Python loop bounded by ~104 fixtures does not rise to a "medium" issue. Lowering to info; marking not-real (falsePositive) because the concern is negligible and non-actionable under the stated threat model, per the rules of engagement that default to refuted when an existing pattern is harmless at this scale.

refuted.title: Trajectory and highlights endpoints each trigger a full live-leaderboard recompute on cache miss
refuted.whyRefuted:
The two cited code facts are accurate but the finding's distinguishing claim — a dashboard "fan-out" that multiplies cold leaderboard rebuilds — is refuted by the actual frontend code.

VERIFIED-TRUE pieces:
- backend/app/api/leaderboard.py:154 — `_build_trajectory` does call `live = await calculate_leaderboard(session, phase=None)`, used by /snapshots/me and /snapshots/{user_id}.
- backend/app/services/profile.py:20-23 — `calculate_user_stats` calls both `calculate_user_points` and `calculate_leaderboard(session)` (overall key), powering /auth/me/stats and /users/{id}/profile.
- A cold rebuild is genuinely expensive: leaderboard.py:138-156 loops over all active users calling `calculate_user_points` (scoring.py:575) which itself runs a per-finished-fixture `get_outcome_counts` query plus advancement/group-bonus/bonus queries — O(users x fixtures). And there is no asyncio single-flight lock around the cache (leaderboard.py:96-193). So PERF-1 (cold rebuild) and PERF-2 (no single-flight) are real underlying issues.

REFUTED — the load-bearing fan-out premise is false:
1. "the dashboard fans out: it can request a sparkline per leaderboard row" — FALSE. The leaderboard page's per-row sparkline uses the CLIENT-SIDE stub `stubRankTrajectory(r.user_id, ...)` (frontend/src/routes/leaderboard/+page.svelte:204), and PnSparkline accepts pre-computed ranks; it makes no API call. The dashboard standings widget (DwTop5) reuses the already-fetched leaderboard store. A grep of the entire frontend shows `getUserTrajectory` (per-row /snapshots/{user_id}) is never called anywhere.
2. "plus climbers" — FALSE. `getClimbers` is never called from the frontend (grep: zero hits in frontend/src), and the /climbers endpoint does not call calculate_leaderboard anyway — get_steepest_climbers (snapshots.py:105-149) reads only LeaderboardSnapshot rows.
3. Each of the four dashboards (DashGroupStage:43, DashboardKO:54, DashboardBetween:49, DashboardPost:60) calls `getMyRankTrajectory()` exactly ONCE for the user's own trajectory, and that call shares the same `overall` cache key as the dashboard's own `getLeaderboard`/`fetchLeaderboard`. So one dashboard load touches calculate_leaderboard via at most 2-3 calls, all on the same cache entry — there is no per-row multiplication.

Net: the genuine residual ("a trajectory/profile request landing on a TTL boundary forces the cold rebuild") is purely a restatement of PERF-1 + PERF-2, which the finding itself concedes "largely neutralizes this." It introduces no independent issue, and its differentiating claim plus its recommendation (build a batch sparkline endpoint to cut per-row chattiness) target a fan-out that does not exist in this codebase. Under the stated threat model (cheating/snooping/integrity for ~30 friends behind Cloudflare) this is pure performance with zero security/integrity impact. Not a real standalone finding; downgraded from low to info/false-positive.

refuted.title: Predictions wizard subscribes to a 1-second countdown ticker, scheduling a flush every second on a heavy component tree
refuted.whyRefuted:
The cited code is accurate. frontend/src/lib/stores/phase.ts:17-26 defines `currentTime = readable(new Date(), ...)` ticking every 1000ms via setInterval, and lines 61-69 derive `phase1Countdown`/`phase2Countdown` from `[deadline, currentTime]`. The predictions wizard at frontend/src/routes/predictions/+page.svelte:810/812/892/894 renders `{$phase1Countdown ?? '—'}`/`{$phase2Countdown ?? '—'}` inline in both desktop and mobile hero, so it does subscribe to a 1Hz store. grep confirms `currentTime`/`phase1Countdown`/`phase2Countdown` appear ONLY as template expressions (plus the import), never inside any `$:` block.

I verified the heavy reactive blocks named in the finding: `livePredictionMap` (+page.svelte:245) depends on `$matchPredictions`/`$unsavedChanges`; `standingsResult` (:266) on `$groupFixtures`/`livePredictionMap`; `thirdPlaceResult` (:350) on `standingsMap`. None reference `currentTime`. Because of Svelte's per-statement reactivity, a tick only updates the two countdown text nodes plus flush overhead — the expensive standings/tiebreaker chains do NOT re-run. The finding's own analysis reaches this exact conclusion, calls the cost negligible/minor, and recommends leaving it as-is.

Verdict: REFUTED as an actionable issue. (1) It is entirely outside this audit's threat model, which is about cheating/tampering, snooping/IDOR, server-side blind-pool enforcement, data integrity, and basic web hygiene — not frontend render performance. A 1Hz client-side text update cannot let a friend cheat, snoop, or corrupt data. (2) Even on performance grounds it is a self-acknowledged non-problem: the author concedes the cost is bounded/minor, recommends doing nothing, and the only stated concern is hypothetical ("if any future refactor makes a heavy block transitively depend on currentTime"). A speculative note about a refactor that does not exist is not a real defect. Adjusted to info: the observation is technically correct but carries no security/integrity weight and describes no current performance defect.

refuted.title: Whole app is client-rendered (ssr=false), so first paint waits on JS download + parse
refuted.whyRefuted:
The cited code is verified accurate. /Users/lukeaarohi/pyfiles/predictorv2/.claude/worktrees/ultra/frontend/src/routes/+layout.ts:3 contains exactly `export const ssr = false;`, and it is the only ssr/csr/prerender declaration in the entire routes tree (grep found no per-route overrides), so it genuinely makes the whole app a client-only SPA. The stated rationale also checks out: +layout.svelte:24-32 runs `initAuth()` inside `onMount`, and the auth store reads a JWT from localStorage — which SSR cannot access — so client-rendering is a defensible deliberate choice. The PERF-1 amplifier it references is also real: flag bundle files exist (frontend/src/lib/utils/flagSvgs.ts, flags.ts).

So the technical CLAIM is correct. However, this is NOT a real finding under THIS audit's threat model. The threat model is scoped to security/integrity for ~30 competitive friends: cheating (editing after lock, tampering with score/paid/admin flags), snooping (server-side blind pool / IDOR), data integrity (lost/corrupted predictions, shifted lock timing), and basic web hygiene (secrets, CORS, headers, injection, takeover). Client-side rendering of an authenticated app behind Cloudflare has zero bearing on any of these. It does not let a friend read another user's predictions (the blind pool must hold server-side regardless of SSR/CSR), does not weaken lock enforcement (invariant 2 is server-time enforced), does not affect data integrity, and exposes no secret. The author themselves rates it `info`, states 'No action required for launch,' and frames it as a marginal first-paint note — i.e. a pure frontend-performance observation, which is outside the security/integrity remit and not an actionable bug. Per the rules of engagement (default to refuted when the concern does not genuinely matter under the threat model and is not a real bug), I mark isReal=false while leaving severity at the author's correct self-assessment of info. It is an accurate performance note, not a security or data-integrity finding.
